title: Deep modules
description: How to judge where a boundary belongs: a module's interface is a cost paid by every caller; its hidden functionality is the benefit. Pay the interface cost only when the hidden work justifies it.
body:
**The principle.** A module's interface is a cost paid by every caller, so pay it only when the functionality it hides justifies it.

## Why

A module's **interface** is a cost paid by every caller (names to learn, invariants to preserve, parameters to thread); the **functionality** it hides is the benefit. A **shallow module** — wide public surface relative to the work it does — buys nothing: the boundary adds learning cost without hiding meaningful complexity. A **deep module** — small public surface, large hidden implementation — lets callers see one concept and trust it.

## How to apply

Run the **deletion test**: imagine deleting the module. If complexity vanishes, it was a pass-through and the boundary was not earning its keep. If complexity reappears across N callers, it was earning its keep. Pay the interface cost only when the hidden functionality justifies it.
provenance: Attribution: Ousterhout, via Matt Pocock. Imported from v1.

title: Edit-first curation
description: Edit is the default; authoring a new artifact is the justified exception. Search before you write.
body:
**The pattern.** Edit is the default; authoring a new artifact is the justified exception, taken only after searching for what already exists.

## Problem

Duplicate artifacts split authority — a consumer does not know which one to trust — and a fresh artifact severs the revision history and evidence chain that would otherwise stay attached to the original.

## Approach

Edit the closest existing artifact by default. Writing a new one must be justified: state what search terms were run, what the closest existing artifact was, and why editing it was not the right move. Search-before-write is the cheapest duplication defence there is.

## Tradeoffs

You trade the up-front cost of searching and of bending an existing artifact to fit, against the downstream cost of split authority and a broken evidence chain. Reaching for a new artifact is faster in the moment but leaves consumers unsure which source is canonical.
provenance: Imported from v1.

title: Assess tradeoffs by naming both sides
description: Every tradeoff surfaced must answer “what are we trading — A vs B?” with both sides in concrete, user-facing terms.
body:
**The pattern.** When surfacing a tradeoff, name both sides — "what are we trading? — A vs B" — in concrete, user-facing terms.

## Problem

Generic "more work" / "more complex" framings do not actually expose a tradeoff: in an AI-coded corpus, cascade work amortises, so the only durable "more work" cost is AI-illegible complexity that guidance and tooling cannot solve.

## Approach

For any tradeoff surfaced, state both sides explicitly in concrete user-facing terms: latency, blast radius, contract strength, observability, security posture, reversibility. A framing that names only a vague cost does not satisfy the rule.

## Tradeoffs

You trade the effort of articulating both sides precisely against decisions made on vague, unfalsifiable cost language. Naming A vs B in real terms is more work to write but makes the choice auditable.
provenance: Imported from v1.

title: Prove-it gate
description: A unit reaches `healthy` only via earned, on-disk evidence — never a hand-edit.
body:
**The principle.** A unit reaches `healthy` only through earned, on-disk evidence produced by one of its proof modes — never a hand-edit.

## Why

Without an evidence gate, `healthy` becomes a claim rather than a proof — a unit can be marked done by assertion, and the trunk silently accumulates unproven work. The gate **refuses** invalid work rather than warning about it, so an unproven unit cannot pass at all.

## How to apply

Ask: is there earned, on-disk evidence from one of this unit's proof modes at HEAD? If not, it is not `healthy`, and no hand-edit can make it so. Corollary — **cold-rebuild** (an authoring guideline, not a gate): a story should be written self-contained enough that a cold agent — from the story's spec plus its upstream stories' declared interfaces, and nothing else — could rebuild it and pass its UAT (the internals may differ). It is not the definition of `healthy` and is never machine-enforced (ADR-0010 §6).

title: Proof mode
description: The four ways a unit earns `healthy`, one rung per tier — UAT, integration-test, contract-test, and operator-attested.
body:
**In one line.** The four ways a unit earns `healthy` — one rung per tier — comprising UAT, integration-test, contract-test, and operator-attested.

## What it is

The mechanism by which a unit earns `healthy`, one rung per tier (ADR-0010). `packages/proof-protocol` encodes these as a discriminated `proof_mode` union (ADR-0007). The four modes are:

- **UAT** (story) — an honest scripted acceptance walkthrough against *real* collaborators, proving the whole organism end to end.

- **integration-test** (capability) — the organs wired against *real in-story collaborators* (no stubs within the organism).

- **contract-test** (contract) — one isolated automated assertion (collaborators stubbed; the mock-UAT seam permits it).

- **operator-attested** — a per-unit, operator-granted signed event for surfaces with neither an honest UAT nor an isolatable test. An agent can never self-exempt.

title: Observability-first
description: If a state change isn’t a typed event the UI can render, it doesn’t exist. The event model is designed before features.
body:
**The principle.** If a state change isn't a typed event the UI can render, it doesn't exist — so the event model is designed before features.

## Why

Observability bolted on after the fact leaves state changes invisible: behaviour happens that the studio cannot show, and the system becomes unauditable. The event store is the single source of truth the studio renders; if it is not the foundation, there is no later pass that can reconstruct what was never recorded. No external trace SaaS sits in the loop.

## How to apply

Design the event model **before** features. For every state change — owned-loop events and orchestrator events alike — ensure it is a typed record in the event store. Run the test: if a state change is not an event the UI can render, **it does not exist**.

title: Go slow, own the layers
description: Own every load-bearing layer you must not rent — the event model, the orchestrator spine, concurrency-safe state, your data and traces. Stay model-agnostic by a pivot-out seam, not by refusing a subscription. No vibing the parts the system rests on.
body:
**The principle.** Own every load-bearing layer you must not rent — design it up front, go slow, and never vibe the parts the system rests on — keeping model-agnosticism by a pivot-out seam rather than by self-hosting the runtime.

## Why

This is v2's bet, learned from v1: v1's internals were vibed and became unobservable — vibed load-bearing layers become unobservable and unfixable. v1 proved the *idea*, but its improvised internals are hard to see and hard to change. The parts the system rests on — the event model, the orchestrator spine, concurrency-safe state, and your data and traces — must be designed up front, not improvised, and must stay yours. The runtime is the one load-bearing layer storytree deliberately rents: ADR-0030 makes the live executor the Claude Agent SDK on subscription auth, a bounded, reversible choice. Renting becomes the anti-pattern only without a pivot-out seam, or when the rented runtime is allowed to own your data and traces.

## How to apply

Before building on a layer, ask: is this load-bearing? If so, own it — design it deliberately, go slow, and keep your data, traces, event model, and spine yours. Achieve model-agnosticism through the pivot-out seam (ADR-0030): confine the runtime to a single import site behind the `PhaseAuthor` seam, so the live SDK executor is swappable and the owned API-key loop survives as the offline/deterministic test executor and pivot-out fallback — that is what lets renting the runtime stay a deliberate, bounded choice. The orchestrator owns only what the executor does not — multi-node scheduling and durable, concurrency-safe shared state.

title: Confine model calls to one boundary
description: The model runtime is reached only through packages/agent; only the orchestrator drives it; a run is an event, never a node; the orchestrator is the sole fan-out point.
body:
**The boundary.** Every model call is confined behind one orchestrator-driven boundary, so model-unavailability is a *local* failure, never a system outage.

## Rule

- The model runtime is reached **only** through `packages/agent` — the sole place a model runtime is imported.

- **Only** `packages/orchestrator` drives `packages/agent`; `packages/storage-protocol` and `apps/studio` have no path to a model runtime.

- **Run ≠ node:** an owned-loop run/attempt is an execution event, never a new tree node.

- The orchestrator is the **sole fan-out point** — owned-loop nodes never schedule child nodes.

## Enforced by

A package-boundary dependency rule: `packages/agent` is the only module that may import a model runtime, and only `packages/orchestrator` may import `packages/agent` — `packages/storage-protocol` and `apps/studio` have no build-graph path to either.

## Failure mode prevented

If the boundary is crossed, a model runtime leaks into `packages/storage-protocol` or `apps/studio`, model calls fan out from arbitrary places, and model-unavailability becomes a system-wide outage rather than a single contained failure.

title: The spine sequences, the leaf judges
description: If a for-loop or a match could express the routing, the spine (code) owns it; if the routing needs the model to decide what comes next, the leaf (owned-loop node) owns it.
body:
**The principle.** If a for-loop or a match could express the routing, the spine (code) owns it; if the routing needs the model to decide what comes next, the leaf (owned-loop node) owns it.

## Why

Without this discriminator, routing decisions leak into the model that a deterministic for-loop or match could have made — making control-flow non-reproducible, unauditable, and needlessly costly — or, inversely, genuine judgement gets hard-coded into a brittle spine. Keeping closed, deterministic routing in code and open judgement in the leaf keeps each where it can be proven.

## How to apply

For each routing decision, ask: could a for-loop or a match express it? If yes, the **spine** owns it — the code-sequenced orchestrator over plain Postgres (DBOS deferred, ADR-0019), with closed, deterministic routing. If the routing needs the model to decide what comes next, the **leaf** (owned-loop node) owns it; the owned loop is the leaf the spine delegates to.
provenance: The discriminator is carried verbatim from v1.

title: Approval-gated trunk
description: Governs the PRODUCT trunk — the story DAG that storytree grows, not the toolmaker repo's git `main`. On the product trunk a green result is meant to surface for human diff-review and land only on operator approval; content invariants (the prove-it gate) are never bypassable. The per-action approval and studio diff-review surfaces are the intended posture, not yet built. The toolmaker repo itself deliberately runs the literal inverse — auto-merge-on-green (ADR-0022) — because for a solo author green is accepted as sufficient.
body:
**The boundary.** On the product trunk, a green result is a request for human diff-review, not an automatic merge — the trunk is landed on only by operator approval, and content invariants are never bypassable.

## Rule

Scope: this governs the **product** story-trunk (ADR-0008). It does **not** govern the toolmaker repo's git `main`, which deliberately auto-merges on green for a solo author (ADR-0022) — the inverse posture, sharing only the non-negotiable *never merge un-green* invariant.

- **Content invariants are never bypassable (BUILT).** Contracts green, UAT signed, upstream healthy — enforced spine-side by the prove-it gate; there is no code path that lands un-proven work.

- **Per-action approval (INTENDED, not built).** The aspiration is to approve / reject / steer individual in-loop actions, inverting v1's skip-permissions. The studio does not yet drive the loop at this grain.

- **Approval-gated landing (PARTIAL).** As built (ADR-0031), a signed REAL pass auto-pushes a `claude/real/<unit>-<run>` branch and surfaces `gh pr create` in the build envelope — the operator's one human decision per landing is *opening the PR*; the PR then auto-merges on green. The richer diff-review-then-approve gate is intended, not yet built.

## Enforced by

Two layers, honestly split between built and intended. BUILT: the prove-it gate (ADR-0020) enforces content invariants spine-side — a landing can never carry un-proven work, and that floor is not bypassable. PARTIAL: promotion (ADR-0031) parks the proven commit on `claude/real/<unit>-<run>`, pushes it, and surfaces `gh pr create` as the operator step — opening the PR is the one human decision per landing, after which the toolmaker repo's CI auto-merges on green (ADR-0022). NOT YET BUILT: a studio diff-review gate that holds the green result for explicit operator approve/reject/steer before it lands; until that exists, the human's lever is whether to open (or `hold`/draft) the PR, not a per-diff approval surface.

## Failure mode prevented

If the boundary is crossed, knowingly-broken or unreviewed work lands on the product trunk and human judgment is removed from the outer loop. This is exactly v1's **auto-merge-on-green** scar: v1's trunk auto-merged the moment tests went green and tolerated broken intermediate states under an eventual-consistency posture, so the mainline was knowingly-broken at times — buying landing throughput at the cost of a trunk you could not trust. The product trunk inverts it: a green result is a *request for human diff-review*, never an automatic merge, so it never holds a knowingly-broken state. (The toolmaker repo accepts auto-merge-on-green deliberately, per ADR-0022 — green is sufficient there because there is no second reviewer; that is a scoped exception, not a crossed boundary.) The diff-review step is the price paid to close the window in which a trunk holds broken states.

title: Claims live in the shared store
description: Write-ownership is a typed claim checked at node-schedule time in the one shared store; a conflict is a hard refusal, never a warning.
body:
**The boundary.** Write-ownership is a typed claim checked at node-schedule time in the one shared store, and a conflict is a hard refusal — never a warning. (The claim layer is the intended design; it waits on the deferred DBOS path — ADR-0019.)

## Rule

Coordination moves off git and into the one shared Postgres store. The intended mechanism stands on DBOS, which is deferred (ADR-0019), so the claim layer is not built yet — the Phase-2 library migration ran on a plain typed Postgres connection and did not need it.

- A **claim** is a typed write-ownership record naming what a node intends to write, checked under a serializable/unique constraint at **node-schedule time**.

- A conflict is a **hard refusal** (a `claim-conflict-refused` event), never a warning.

- DBOS workflow isolation would replace branch-per-session for coordination, and DB-allocated ids dissolve the collision classes — both part of the deferred DBOS path.

## Enforced by

When built, a serializable/unique constraint on the claims table in the shared Postgres store, checked at node-schedule time: a conflicting claim raises a `claim-conflict-refused` event instead of committing, so two nodes can never hold the same write. This is the intended enforcement; it waits on the deferred DBOS path (ADR-0019).

## Failure mode prevented

If the boundary is crossed once concurrent multi-node work lands, two nodes hold the same write concurrently and corrupt shared state. This is v1’s **store-lock-races-and-id-collisions** scar: once work ran concurrently, v1 hit store-lock races and in-process story-ID collisions (even duplicate ADR numbers), because concurrency-safety was retrofitted rather than designed in. v2 treats concurrency-safe state as a *foundation, not a retrofit* — the claim checked at node-schedule time and DB-allocated collision-free ids dissolve both the race and the collision classes. The race and collision classes are simply not yet exercised: the intended remedy stands on the deferred DBOS path (ADR-0019), so until concurrent multi-node work lands on the plain typed Postgres store the claim layer is named/intended, not built.
provenance: The DBOS-backed mechanism is deferred by ADR-0019.

title: TypeScript · Node 24 · pnpm workspaces
description: The language and runtime: TypeScript on Node 24, organised as pnpm workspaces. Model-agnostic, owns the loop.
body:
**The choice.** The language and runtime: TypeScript on Node 24, organised as pnpm workspaces.

## What it is

storytree is a TypeScript / Node 24 / pnpm-workspaces monorepo. TypeScript gives typed boundaries and invalid-state modelling across the organism packages (e.g. `packages/proof-protocol`, `packages/library`); pnpm workspaces keep `packages/*` and `apps/*` independent but linked. It is model-agnostic and owns the loop.

## Why this

Chosen over v1's Rust for model-agnostic, own-the-loop development speed.

## Constraints

Node 24 is the pinned runtime. Workspace structure: `packages/*` and `apps/*` are kept independent but linked.

title: The owned per-node agent loop
description: We build the per-node leaf runtime ourselves — a minimal tool-use loop on the Anthropic Messages API (`@anthropic-ai/sdk`), living in `packages/agent`.
body:
**The choice.** The per-node leaf runtime is owned code — a minimal tool-use loop on the Anthropic Messages API, living in `packages/agent`.

## What it is

The owned agent loop runs everything *inside* a node — the model loop, steering, diffs, approvals. It is a minimal tool-use loop on the Anthropic Messages API (`@anthropic-ai/sdk`): `messages.create` with `tools` → dispatch `tool_use` → feed `tool_result` → loop to `end_turn`, emitting a lifecycle event stream plus `edit`-tool diffs. It lives in `packages/agent` (ADR-0011 replaced the third-party `pi` leaf with this owned loop).

## Why this

Context engineering and the agent loop are the layers storytree most needs to own and see into — renting them means renting exactly what we need to control. ADR-0011 retired the earlier bet on a third-party `pi` leaf in favour of this owned loop, for that reason; `packages/pi-adapter` is a dead placeholder and the `pi-` ids here are residual rename debt, not a live `pi` dependency. The v1 Claude Code SDK opacity (an opaque wrapper around a binary) is the cautionary tale. Anthropic-only for now behind a thin internal seam; the earlier model-agnostic non-negotiable is relaxed to a *revisit-if-it-bites* posture, not re-committed (ADR-0011).

## Constraints

The model runtime is reached **only** through `packages/agent`, the sole model-runtime import site; `packages/storage-protocol` and `apps/studio` have no path to it (ADR-0004, amended by ADR-0011).

title: DBOS (Transact-TS) over Postgres
description: Durable execution — crash-safe concurrent workflows, auto-resume, durable queues — the named/reserved future target, deferred (ADR-0019); the store is a plain typed node-pg Postgres connection now.
body:
**The choice.** DBOS (Transact-TS) over Postgres is the named, reserved durable-execution target — crash-safe concurrent workflows, auto-resume, durable queues — but it is deferred (ADR-0019); the store is a plain typed node-pg Postgres connection today.

## What it is

DBOS would give crash-safe parallelism as a library, not a cluster: auto-resumed workflows, durable queues with concurrency caps, and collision-free workflow ids. It is deferred (ADR-0019, reaffirmed ADR-0020): today the store is a plain typed `node-pg` Postgres connection and the orchestrator runs over it directly — no DBOS, no durable workflows. DBOS stays the named/reserved target for what the orchestrator owns and the owned loop does not: multi-node scheduling and durable, concurrency-safe shared state.

## Why this

Durable execution as a library rather than a cluster is the reason it is held as the future target. It was deferred because the Phase-2 library migration ran fine on a plain typed Postgres connection and did not need durable workflows yet (ADR-0019). Restate is the reserved alternative if a single self-contained binary is later preferred over a Postgres dependency.

## Constraints

Deferred, not adopted: nothing depends on DBOS today, and the store is a plain typed `node-pg` Postgres connection. When taken up it carries a Postgres dependency (the reason Restate is held in reserve) and would back durable, concurrency-safe shared state and multi-node scheduling — the layer the orchestrator owns.
provenance: Deferred by ADR-0019 (reaffirmed ADR-0020).

title: PixiJS v8 + @pixi/react studio
description: The studio is a React web IDE with an embedded PixiJS 2D-isometric tree (deferred): fastest 2D, batches 1000s of live sprites at 60fps.
body:
**The choice.** PixiJS v8 + @pixi/react: a React web IDE with an embedded PixiJS 2D-isometric tree (deferred).

## What it is

The studio is a React shell embedding a PixiJS v8 (`@pixi/react`) 2D-isometric tree that renders the event store live. PixiJS is the fastest 2D engine and batches thousands of live sprites at 60fps. Art is deferred; the engine is settled.

## Why this

Fastest 2D, batches 1000s of live sprites at 60fps — the throughput a live-rendered tree of the event store needs.

## Constraints

PixiJS is pinned to **v8** (via `@pixi/react`). **Note:** this foundation build ships the React forum shell only — no PixiJS yet; the engine is chosen but not yet integrated.

title: The gate is never bypassable
description: Content invariants — contracts green, UAT signed, upstream healthy — can never be bypassed; the gate refuses invalid work rather than warning.
body:
**The boundary.** The content invariants — contracts green, UAT signed, upstream healthy — can never be bypassed; the gate refuses invalid work rather than warning about it.

## Rule

A **gate** is a structural enforcement point that **refuses** invalid work, not a warning. Promotion onto the trunk requires its content invariants — contracts green, UAT signed, upstream healthy — and these are **never bypassable**. An operator approval admits work that has *already* passed the gate; it cannot waive it.

## Enforced by

The gate is the sole writer of trunk-promotion events and emits one only when every content invariant holds; the operator-approval check runs *after* the invariants and has no branch that can waive them.

## Failure mode prevented

If the boundary is crossed, work that fails its content invariants reaches the trunk — an operator (or any path) waiving the gate rather than merely admitting already-passing work, so the trunk holds unproven or broken units.

title: An agent can never self-exempt
description: operator-attested promotion is operator-granted only; an agent can never grant itself the attestation that reaches `healthy`.
body:
**The boundary.** An agent can never grant itself the attestation that reaches `healthy` — operator-attested promotion is operator-granted only.

## Rule

Attestation and proof are separate claims kept in separate logs (ADR-0044): a per-UAT-test attestation — a human vouch or a machine run — is an append-only signal in `events.attestation`, keyed by test id, that NEVER writes to `events.verdict` and never rolls up to a story-level hue. The only thing that reaches `healthy` is a signed Verdict, and an agent can **never** self-attest one; `operator-attested` (ADR-0007) remains a distinct, human-anchored signed mode.

## Enforced by

Two real, deterministic mechanisms keep an agent from minting its own promotion to `healthy`. (1) Attestations live in a separate **non-proof** log (`events.attestation`, ADR-0044): keyed by test id, never written to `events.verdict`, with no story roll-up — so a self-signed attestation, even one the agent relayed and scribed, cannot move any unit to `healthy`. (2) The only thing that reaches `healthy` is a signed Verdict, which the **spine** signs out-of-band in the `GATE` phase *after* it has itself observed RED then GREEN via an executor (ADR-0020 §3–4); the leaf never reports its own verdict, so authoring and signing stay separate authorities. NOT YET BUILT (a candidate belt-and-suspenders follow-up): `signer.ts` resolves *an* identity but never compares it to the agent under test, `attestations.ts` records `signer`/`relayedBy` as provenance but enforces no distinctness, and there is no operator-attested branch in the gate — so the literal "reject an attestation signed by the agent under test" check does not exist; the spirit holds today via the two mechanisms above, not via signer-distinctness.

## Failure mode prevented

If the boundary is crossed, an agent self-exempts — minting its own `operator-attested` promotion to `healthy` for a surface with no honest UAT or isolatable test, defeating the proof model.

title: A run is not a node
description: An owned-loop run/attempt is an execution event (many per node), never a new tree node. The execution environment is not the coordination structure.
body:
**The boundary.** An owned-loop run/attempt is an execution event (many per node), never a new tree node — the execution environment is not the coordination structure.

## Rule

An owned-loop **run** (attempt) is recorded as an execution **event**, many-per-node — it is **never** a new node on the DAG. The coordination/scheduling grain (the node) is kept distinct from the execution grain (the run).

## Enforced by

The event-store schema (`packages/store/src/schema.sql`): a run is a `run`-typed row in the event store keyed to its node id, with no node identity of its own and no slot in the node table for one to occupy.

## Failure mode prevented

If the boundary is crossed, execution attempts proliferate as DAG nodes, conflating the execution grain with the coordination/scheduling grain and corrupting the tree the orchestrator schedules over.

title: The orchestrator is the sole fan-out point
description: Only the orchestrator schedules nodes; owned-loop nodes never schedule child nodes (no agent-spawns-agent).
body:
**The boundary.** Only the orchestrator schedules nodes; owned-loop nodes never schedule child nodes — there is no agent-spawns-agent path.

## Rule

The orchestrator is the **only** module that drives `packages/agent` and the **sole fan-out point** — it schedules nodes. An owned-loop node never schedules child nodes; there is no agent-spawns-agent path.

## Enforced by

Only `packages/orchestrator` holds the schedule-node capability; the `packages/agent` surface exposes no scheduling call upward, so an owned-loop node has no API by which to spawn a child node.

## Failure mode prevented

If the boundary is crossed, an owned-loop node spawns child nodes (agent-spawns-agent), fan-out escapes the orchestrator’s deterministic spine, and scheduling becomes uncontrolled.

title: Thin wrapper over the runtime
description: Own a thin, typed wrapper over the agent runtime’s documented surface; expose nothing model-shaped upward.
body:
**The pattern.** Own a thin, typed wrapper over the agent runtime's documented surface, and expose nothing model-shaped upward.

## Problem

If `core` and `studio` parsed a model runtime's raw stream directly, every consumer would couple to an undocumented, model-shaped surface that can shift underneath them.

## Approach

The owned loop lives in one package, `packages/agent` — the **sole** model-runtime import site. It exposes the typed surface (`prompt` / `steer` / `follow-up` + lifecycle stream + `edit` diffs), normalizes the owned loop's stream into the typed events the store renders, and lets nothing model-shaped leak upward — `core` and `studio` never parse the owned loop's stream directly.

## Tradeoffs

You trade the cost of building and maintaining a normalization layer against direct, brittle coupling to the owned loop's stream. The wrapper is extra code, but it confines every model-shaped change to one place.
provenance: Carries v1's own-a-thin-wrapper-over-the-agent-runtime principle.

title: Event log, then projection
description: The append-only event log is the only thing written; per-unit status/verdict is a derived projection (node rollup), never hand-maintained.
body:
**The pattern.** Write only the append-only event log; derive per-unit status and verdict as a projection (node rollup) read off it, never hand-maintained.

## Problem

Maintaining current status beside the log invites the per-build `runs`-grain mess v1 fell into, where derived state drifts from the record it is supposed to reflect.

## Approach

The **event log** is the typed, append-only record — the single source of truth and the only thing **written**. The **node rollup** (current status + latest verdict per unit) is a **projection** over the log, *read off* it, never written beside it.

## Tradeoffs

You trade the convenience of reading a stored status directly against the integrity of a single source of truth. Projecting on read costs computation, but rules out the drift that comes from maintaining status separately from the log.
provenance: v2's answer to v1's per-build `runs`-grain mess.

title: One durable workflow per node
description: Each node is a single owned-loop session; the intended durable path drives it inside one DBOS workflow (crash-safe, auto-resumed), but DBOS is deferred (ADR-0019/0020) — today the spine runs the session over plain Postgres with best-effort replay.
body:
**The pattern.** Drive each node as a single owned-loop session, isolation keyed on the node. The intended durable path wraps it in one DBOS workflow (crash-safe, auto-resumed), but DBOS is deferred (ADR-0019, reaffirmed ADR-0020) — today the spine runs the session over plain Postgres with best-effort replay.

## Problem

A node under construction needs resumable execution and write-ownership isolation, without resorting to a branch-per-session model for coordination.

## Approach

A node under construction is driven by one owned-loop session. The intended durable mechanism wraps that session in a DBOS workflow — DBOS workflow isolation (not a branch-per-session) providing crash-safe, auto-resumed execution — but DBOS is deferred (ADR-0019), so today the spine drives the session over a plain typed Postgres connection with best-effort replay rather than guaranteed auto-resume; claims in the shared store (also deferred with DBOS) would handle write-ownership.

## Tradeoffs

The durable-workflow path buys crash recovery and auto-resume at the cost of standing on DBOS and the shared store rather than git branches; until DBOS is taken up, the plain-Postgres spine accepts best-effort replay in exchange for not carrying that dependency yet.
provenance: The DBOS workflow path is deferred by ADR-0019 (reaffirmed ADR-0020).

title: Standalone-resilient library
description: Structure a unit as a library with minimal load-bearing deps, exercised end-to-end by integration tests, behind a thin CLI/adapter wrapper.
body:
**The pattern.** Structure a unit as a library with minimal load-bearing dependencies, exercised end-to-end by integration tests, behind a thin CLI/adapter wrapper.

## Problem

Units that are tangled into their surroundings cannot be proven in isolation and break whenever the surrounding code churns.

## Approach

Build each unit as a library with minimal load-bearing dependencies, exercised end-to-end by integration tests, behind a thin CLI/adapter wrapper.

## Tradeoffs

You trade the discipline of keeping dependencies minimal and wrapping the library thinly against tighter, more convenient coupling to the surroundings. The library shape keeps the unit provable in isolation and resilient to surrounding churn.
provenance: Carried from v1.

title: Faked-UAT theatre
description: Stubbing collaborators in a UAT is a structural defect — the mock-UAT seam forbids it. Guardrail surfaces use operator-attested, not a fake walkthrough.
body:
**The pattern.** Never fake a walkthrough — stubbing collaborators in a UAT is a structural defect; surfaces with no honest UAT use the operator-attested proof mode instead.

## Problem

Stubs/mocks are correct in a contract test (isolated) but a **structural defect** in a UAT, which must run against real collaborators. Behavioural/guardrail surfaces often have no honest UAT, tempting a faked walkthrough.

## Approach

Don't fake a walkthrough for a behavioural/guardrail surface that has no honest UAT — use the `operator-attested` proof mode instead. The mock-UAT seam draws this line: mocks belong in isolated contract tests, not in a UAT.

## Tradeoffs

You trade the false comfort of a green-looking but stubbed walkthrough against an honest signal. Operator-attestation is more work and requires a human, but a faked UAT proves nothing about the real organism.

title: When a term is in question, the glossary wins
description: docs/glossary.md is the authoritative terminology every layer speaks; when a term’s meaning is contested, it wins.
body:
**The pattern.** When a term's meaning is in question, `docs/glossary.md` is authoritative — it wins, and the reasoning lives in the cited ADR.

## Problem

With multiple layers each speaking the same vocabulary, a contested term can mean different things in different places unless one source is named authoritative.

## Approach

Every layer — the organism packages' types, the orchestrator, the studio, the ADRs — uses the glossary's terms as defined. When a term's meaning is in question, `docs/glossary.md` wins; the reasoning behind the definition lives in the cited ADR.

## Tradeoffs

You trade local freedom to redefine a term against a single authoritative vocabulary. Deferring to the glossary constrains how a layer may use a word, but guarantees every layer speaks the same language when it matters.

title: The human owns the outer loop
description: Inner loop (drive a unit red→green) is automatable; outer loop (accept to trunk, accept a decomposition, amend/retry/abandon) is human judgment.
body:
**The boundary.** The outer loop — accepting a result onto the trunk, accepting a decomposition, amending/retrying/abandoning a unit — is human judgment, never an automated path.

## Rule

**inner loop** = driving one unit red→green (automatable, owned by an owned-loop node). **outer loop** = accepting a result onto the trunk, accepting a decomposition, or amending/retrying/abandoning a unit (held by **human judgment** in the studio). The human-in-the-loop gate sits at the outer loop; the north-star may later dissolve it.

## Enforced by

The outer-loop transitions (accept-to-trunk, accept-decomposition, amend / retry / abandon) are operator-only actions in the studio, each recorded as an operator-signed event; the orchestrator exposes no automated path that performs them.

## Failure mode prevented

If the boundary is crossed, an agent performs an outer-loop transition automatically — accepting its own result onto the trunk or its own decomposition — removing human judgment from the loop the human is meant to own.

title: Template — definition
description: Fillable scaffold for a new definition artifact (what something is).
body:
**In one line.** _What this term means, stated once — genus and differentia._

## What it is

_The precise meaning: the category it belongs to and what distinguishes it within that category. Be exact._

## What it is not

_The nearest neighbours it must not be confused with, and the distinction. Omit this section if the term has no easily-confused neighbour._

title: Template — principle
description: Fillable scaffold for a new principle artifact (how to judge).
body:
**The principle.** _The judgement rule, in one sentence._

## Why

_What goes wrong without it — the cost it pays for._

## How to apply

_What following it looks like in practice: the test you run, the question you ask._

title: Template — pattern
description: Fillable scaffold for a new pattern artifact (a reusable approach).
body:
**The pattern.** _The reusable approach, in one sentence._

## Problem

_The recurring situation this addresses._

## Approach

_The structure to apply — the shape or the steps._

## Tradeoffs

_What you trade — A vs B — in concrete, user-facing terms._

title: Template — guardrail
description: Fillable scaffold for a new guardrail artifact — requires an "Enforced by" section.
body:
**The boundary.** _The line that must not be crossed, in one sentence._

## Rule

_The invariant, stated as a hard boundary._

## Enforced by

_The deterministic mechanism that makes this non-bypassable — a gate, a schema, a DB constraint, or a specific code path. If nothing deterministically enforces it, this is a `pattern`, not a guardrail._

## Failure mode prevented

_What breaks if the boundary is crossed._

title: Template — techstack
description: Fillable scaffold for a new techstack artifact (what we build on).
body:
**The choice.** _What we build on, in one sentence._

## What it is

_The technology and the role it plays in storytree._

## Why this

_What it buys us; what it was chosen over._

## Constraints

_Version pins, boundaries, and what it must not be used for._

title: Template — adr
description: Scaffold for a new ADR under docs/decisions/ — the source layer the knowledge tier derives from (ADR-0017), not a knowledge-unit kind. Section shape: Status / Date / Context / Decision / Consequences / Alternatives considered / References.
body:
_Use this to author a new ADR under `docs/decisions/`. ADRs are the **source layer** the knowledge tier derives from (ADR-0017), not a Library knowledge-unit kind — this is the only `template` whose output is a doc, not a `definition` / `principle` / `pattern` / `guardrail` / `techstack` unit._

# ADR-NNNN: <short imperative title>

## Status

_proposed · accepted · superseded by ADR-XXXX_

## Date

_YYYY-MM-DD_

## Context

_The forces at play — what makes this decision necessary now, and the constraints it must satisfy._

## Decision

_What we are doing, stated plainly in the present tense._

## Consequences

_What follows — the trade-offs accepted, the new constraints, what gets easier or harder._

## Alternatives considered

_What else was on the table and why it lost (name both sides of each trade)._

## References

_Source / related ADRs, glossary terms, and Library artifacts._

title: story
description: The top-level unit of work you watch grow, and a node on the DAG the studio renders.
body:
**In one line.** The top-level unit of work you watch grow — a bounded context / organism, self-contained and independently deployable, composed of capabilities and proven as a whole by integrated UAT.

## What it is

The top-level unit of work you watch grow, and a node on the DAG the studio renders. A **bounded context / organism** — self-contained and **independently deployable** (the microservice grain), *composed of capabilities*. The map grain: the thing a newcomer points at ("the event store", "the tree renderer"). A story is **proven as a whole by ≥1 integrated UAT** (acceptance walkthrough of the organism against real collaborators), not a pure rollup of its capabilities. Inside it capabilities share machinery (DRY is good); across stories behaviour is duplicated, not shared, except through a declared **boundary** (ADR-0010).

## What it is not

Deliberately bigger than v1's story (the unit v1/Agentic called a "story" is now a **capability**). Unlike a pure rollup, a story carries its own integrated UAT rather than deriving its health solely from its capabilities.

title: capability
description: An organ within a story (bounded context): independently viable, proven by ≥1 integration test against real in-story collaborators (no stubs within the organism), and composed of contracts.
body:
**In one line.** An organ within a story (bounded context): independently viable, proven by integration tests against real in-story collaborators, and composed of contracts.

## What it is

An **organ within a story** (bounded context): independently viable, proven by ≥1 **integration test** against **real in-story collaborators** (no stubs within the organism), and composed of **contracts**. The within-story dependencies are drawn between capabilities (code-derived; see **dependency**). This is the unit v1 (Agentic) called a "story".

## What it is not

It no longer carries the UAT — that moved up to the story. A unit is a capability — not a **contract** — if it is an integration-wired organ rather than a single isolated behaviour (ADR-0010).

title: contract
description: A single test-proven behaviour within a capability: one automated, isolated unit test (collaborators stubbed).
body:
**In one line.** A single test-proven behaviour within a capability — the leaf of the proof ladder, proven by one automated, isolated unit test (collaborators stubbed).

## What it is

A single **test-proven behaviour** within a capability: one automated, **isolated** unit test (collaborators stubbed). The leaf — the bottom rung of the proof ladder (unit → integration → UAT).

## What it is not

A unit is a contract — not a **capability** — if the only honest proof is an isolated automated assertion, with no wired-up organ around it.

title: node
description: A unit being worked on the DAG — a story or capability under construction — driven by one owned-loop session (the intended DBOS workflow wrapper is deferred, ADR-0019).
body:
**In one line.** A unit being worked on the DAG — a story or capability under construction — driven by one owned-loop session (the intended DBOS workflow wrapper is deferred, ADR-0019).

## What it is

A unit being worked **on the DAG** — a story or capability under construction — driven by one owned-loop session (the intended DBOS workflow wrapper is deferred, ADR-0019). The coordination/scheduling grain: the thing the orchestrator schedules and the isolation/claim layer is keyed on.

## What it is not

Distinct from a **run**: an owned-loop run/attempt against a node is an execution event (many-per-node), never a new node. The execution environment is not the coordination structure (ADR-0004, ADR-0009).

title: run
description: A single per-node execution attempt, recorded as an event in the event store — many-per-node, never a new node.
body:
**In one line.** A single per-node execution attempt, recorded as an event in the event store — many-per-node, never a new node.

## What it is

A single per-node **execution** attempt, recorded as an event in the event store — many-per-node, never a new node. Here `run` is strictly the per-node execution attempt (ADR-0004; see `open-questions.md` §3, §8).

## What it is not

Distinct from v1's overloaded `runs`/`test_runs` table (per-build vs per-event vs id-keyed dir). Distinct from a **node**: a run is the execution grain, not the coordination grain.
provenance: See `open-questions.md` §3, §8.

title: UAT
description: A prose journey, run end-to-end against real collaborators, that proves a story (the whole organism) meets its goal.
body:
**In one line.** A prose journey, run end-to-end against real collaborators, that proves a story (the whole organism) meets its goal.

## What it is

A prose journey, run end-to-end against *real* collaborators, that proves a **story** (the whole organism) meets its goal. Minimal-first: ship one that proves the goal, grow more as defects surface.

## What it is not

Lives at the **story** level only — capabilities get integration tests, contracts get isolated unit tests, not UATs (ADR-0010).

title: contract test
description: The automated test that proves a contract: the isolated unit test at the bottom rung of the proof ladder (unit → integration → UAT).
body:
**In one line.** The automated test that proves a contract — the isolated unit test at the bottom rung of the proof ladder (unit → integration → UAT).

## What it is

The automated test that proves a contract: the **isolated unit test** at the bottom rung of the proof ladder (unit → integration → UAT). Collaborators are stubbed, for fast feedback during the build.

title: dependency
description: A directed edge, defined at two altitudes (ADR-0010).
body:
**In one line.** A directed edge between units, defined at two altitudes: code-derived capability→capability edges within a story, and declared boundaries across stories (ADR-0010).

## What it is

A directed edge, defined at two altitudes (ADR-0010). **Within a story**, capability→capability edges are **code-derived** (static analysis of imports/calls): inside the boundary a dependency *is* the code coupling, read off the source, not hand-authored. **Across stories**, one story may depend on another **only** through a declared, documented **boundary** (§4) — hidden cross-story coupling is forbidden. You cannot prove a unit that stands on an unproven one.

title: boundary
description: The declared, documented seam between two stories — the only legal cross-story coupling: two organisms dependent to deliver an outcome but each still functioning in isolation against the seam, the…
body:
**In one line.** The declared, documented seam between two stories — the only legal cross-story coupling, the way a frontend depends on a database.

## What it is

The declared, documented seam between two stories — the **only** legal cross-story coupling: two organisms dependent to deliver an outcome but each still functioning in isolation against the seam, the way a frontend depends on a database. The seam need not be a code import: a surface may depend on another surface's **delivered build artifact** (consuming its compiled output via a declared cross-story `depends_on` edge) — declared for honesty + forest visibility even though the import-scan gate cannot enforce it, since there is no import to cover (ADR-0100 §3, ADR-0111 §4).

## What it is not

Not a generic `interface`: the name is provisional, because bare `interface` collides with TS `interface` (the same collision that sank `component` in ADR-0002). The candidates are **`boundary`** or **`port`**, to be ratified when `packages/proof-protocol` formalises the schema (ADR-0010 §4).

title: event
description: A typed record of a state change (owned-loop events + orchestrator events) — the unit of observability.
body:
**In one line.** A typed record of a state change (owned-loop events plus orchestrator events) — the unit of observability.

## What it is

A typed record of a state change (owned-loop events + orchestrator events) — the unit of observability. If a state change isn't an event the UI can render, it doesn't exist (ADR-0001, observability-first). Defined alongside the schema in `packages/proof-protocol`. Includes operator-actor events (see **approval event / promotion event**), not just agent activity.

title: event log
description: The typed, append-only record in the event store — one row per state change — that is the single source of truth the studio renders and the only thing written.
body:
**In one line.** The typed, append-only record in the event store — one row per state change — that is the single source of truth the studio renders and the only thing written.

## What it is

The typed, **append-only** record in the event store — one row per state change — that is the single source of truth the studio renders and the only thing **written**. The artifact behind the `event` term.

## What it is not

Distinct from the derived **node rollup**, which is read off the log rather than written (ADR-0006).

title: node rollup
description: The current status and latest verdict per story / capability / contract, derived as a projection over the event log and never hand-maintained.
body:
**In one line.** The current status and latest verdict per story / capability / contract, derived as a projection over the event log and never hand-maintained.

## What it is

The current status and latest `verdict` **per** story / capability / contract, derived as a **projection** over the event log and never hand-maintained. A capability's lifecycle status (proposed / building / healthy; unhealthy computed) is *read off* the log, not written beside it. v2's answer to v1's per-build `runs`-grain mess (ADR-0006).

## What it is not

Distinct from the **event log**: the log is the only thing written, while the rollup is a derived projection over it — never a separately maintained record.

title: owned-loop event stream
description: The owned loop's structured lifecycle event stream (plus edit-tool diffs/patches) emitted as it works inside a node — the agent-activity ingest channel into the event store, normalized by packages/agent.
body:
**In one line.** The owned loop's structured lifecycle event stream (plus edit-tool diffs) emitted as it works inside a node — the agent-activity ingest channel into the event store, normalized by packages/agent.

## What it is

The owned loop's structured lifecycle event stream (plus `edit`-tool diffs/patches) emitted as it works inside a node — the **agent-activity ingest channel** into the event store, normalized by `packages/agent`. One of exactly two defined ingest channels.

## What it is not

Not the only ingest channel: the other is orchestrator events (ADR-0001, ADR-0006, ADR-0011).

title: approval event / promotion event
description: Typed events with actor = operator recorded in the event store: an approval / steering event (a human in-loop intervention) and a signed promotion event (the human accepting a unit's green result o…
body:
**In one line.** Typed operator-actor events in the event store: an approval / steering event (a human in-loop intervention) and a signed promotion event (the human accepting a unit's green result onto the trunk).

## What it is

Typed events with `actor = operator` recorded in the event store: an **approval / steering event** (a human in-loop intervention) and a signed **promotion event** (the human accepting a unit's green result onto the trunk, carrying operator identity and, for a story, the UAT verdict). Part of the same observability record as the owned loop's own activity (ADR-0008).
provenance: Identity backing is open (`open-questions.md` §1).

title: DAG
description: The directed acyclic graph the studio renders and watches grow.
body:
**In one line.** The directed acyclic graph the studio renders and watches grow — stories as visible nodes, capability dependencies as the fine-grained edges beneath.

## What it is

The directed acyclic graph the studio renders and watches grow. Stories are its visible nodes; capability dependencies are the fine-grained edges beneath. The exact inter-level grain is open (see ADR-0002 → "What this does NOT decide").

title: gate
description: A structural enforcement point that refuses invalid work rather than warning.
body:
**In one line.** A structural enforcement point that refuses invalid work rather than warning about it.

## What it is

A structural enforcement point that **refuses** invalid work rather than warning. storytree keeps the commit-time / promotion gate (ADR-0001 cites it as a proven v1 idea).

## What it is not

Not an `approval` (a human admitting work that has already passed the gate's content invariants) and not a mere warning — a gate structurally blocks, it does not advise.

title: operator-attested
description: A proof mode (alongside UAT, integration-test and contract-test) for behavioural/guardrail surfaces that have neither an honest scripted UAT nor an isolatable automated test — e.g.
body:
**In one line.** A proof mode for behavioural/guardrail surfaces with neither an honest scripted UAT nor an isolatable automated test, where promotion to `healthy` is an explicit, per-unit, operator-granted signed event.

## What it is

A proof mode (alongside UAT, integration-test and contract-test) for behavioural/guardrail surfaces that have neither an honest scripted UAT nor an isolatable automated test — e.g. the orchestrator's own routing / approval / steering discipline. Promotion to `healthy` is an explicit, per-unit, **operator-granted** attestation recorded as a typed **signed event**; an agent can never self-exempt, and the attestation is distinguishable in the audit trail from a UAT walkthrough sign.

## What it is not

Distinct from `mapped`, which is a *status* (the authored brownfield state that reaches `healthy` via an observe-and-signed `adopted` verdict, ADR-0085 / ADR-0097 / ADR-0105), not a proof mode. Distinct also from a UAT walkthrough sign, from which it is separable in the audit trail.
provenance: Persistence / identity backing is open (`open-questions.md` §1).

title: convergence
description: Two distinct senses v1 conflated and v2 keeps separate;
body:
**In one line.** A term carrying two distinct senses that v1 conflated and v2 keeps separate — DAG-stabilisation and cold-rebuild — so it must always be qualified.

## What it is

Two **distinct** senses v1 conflated and v2 keeps separate; always qualify which is meant. (1) *DAG-stabilisation* — the dependency DAG is iterated to a fixed point before any unit goes red (owned by the decomposition/scheduler loop; see `open-questions.md` §4). (2) *cold-rebuild* — see the separate `cold-rebuild` definition.

## What it is not

Not a single concept — using it unqualified is the v1 conflation v2 exists to prevent. The two senses are independent: DAG-stabilisation concerns scheduling order, while cold-rebuild concerns story self-containment.
provenance: DAG-stabilisation ownership is open (`open-questions.md` §4).

title: cold-rebuild
description: An authoring guideline (not a gate), at story grain: a story should be self-contained enough that a cold agent could rebuild it from its own spec plus its upstream stories' declared interfaces — and pass its UAT.
body:
**The principle.** A story should be written self-contained enough that a cold agent — given the story's own spec **plus the declared interfaces of its upstream stories** (never their internals), and nothing else — could rebuild it and pass its UAT.

## Why

It is an **authoring guideline** (not a gate), at **story grain**: self-containment is what lets a story be rebuilt and re-proven without spelunking its neighbours' internals. The rebuilt *internals* may legitimately differ — many implementations satisfy one UAT.

## How to apply

When authoring a story, check it could be rebuilt from its own spec + its upstream stories' **declared interfaces** alone. This is the cold-rebuild sense of `convergence`, distinct from DAG-stabilisation. It is **not** the definition of `healthy` (earned via the proof modes / prove-it-gate) and is **not machine-enforced** — v1 carried it as guidance for agents authoring stories and never tested it.
provenance: Carried from Agentic ADR-0006/0027.

title: per-node budget
description: A code-enforced ceiling (iterations / token-cost / wall-cost — exact unit TBD) on a node's spine loop.
body:
**In one line.** A code-enforced ceiling (iterations / token-cost / wall-cost — exact unit TBD) on a node's spine loop.

## What it is

A code-enforced ceiling (iterations / token-cost / wall-cost — exact unit TBD) on a node's spine loop. The loop terminates on green **or** budget-exhausted, the latter a typed terminal event with per-round cost visible in the event store. Resurrected for pay-as-you-go (ADR-0005), inverting v1's "cascade rounds are not a cost".
provenance: The concrete unit and default ceiling stay open (`open-questions.md` §6).

title: approval
description: A first-class, typed operator act (actor = operator) in which the human accepts an agent action, or a capability's green result, onto the trunk via the studio.
body:
**In one line.** A first-class, typed operator act (actor = operator) in which the human accepts an agent action, or a capability's green result, onto the trunk via the studio.

## What it is

A first-class, typed operator act (`actor = operator`) in which the human accepts an agent action, or a capability's green result, **onto the trunk** via the studio. The trunk is **approval-gated**: a green signal is a *request for human diff-review*, not an automatic merge (inverts v1's auto-merge-on-green).

## What it is not

Distinct from a **gate** (which structurally refuses invalid work); an approval is the human admitting work that has already passed the gate's content invariants.
provenance: The identity backing the signature is open (`open-questions.md` §1).

title: verdict
description: The Pass/Fail outcome of a story's UAT.
body:
**In one line.** The Pass/Fail outcome of a story's UAT.

## What it is

The Pass/Fail outcome of a story's UAT. Reserved for UAT outcomes.

## What it is not

v1 also used "verdict" for agent conclusions and evidence-row states — those are different concepts and do not claim the word here.

title: evidence
description: The forensic record that a unit's tests went red→green and its story's UAT was signed: an audit trail, not itself the gate.
body:
**In one line.** The forensic record that a unit's tests went red→green and its story's UAT was signed — an audit trail, not itself the gate.

## What it is

The forensic record that a unit's tests went red→green and its story's UAT was signed: an audit trail.

## What it is not

Not itself the gate — evidence records what happened; the gate is the enforcement point that consumes it.
provenance: How v2 persists evidence (events vs files) and the attestation / identity model are open (`open-questions.md` §1).

title: proof hash
description: A hash of a unit's proof-bearing content (outcome, contracts, …) that invalidates a prior verdict when the content changes.
body:
**In one line.** A hash of a unit's proof-bearing content (outcome, contracts, …) that invalidates a prior verdict when the content changes.

## What it is

A hash of a unit's proof-bearing content (outcome, contracts, …) that invalidates a prior verdict when the content changes.

title: red-green
description: The discipline that a failing (red) contract test is authored before the implementation that turns it green.
body:
**The principle.** A failing (red) contract test is authored before the implementation that turns it green.

## Why

A test that has never been seen to fail is not trusted evidence — writing the implementation first invites tests shaped to pass vacuously. Authoring the test red first proves it actually exercises the behaviour.

## How to apply

Write the contract test, watch it fail (red), then write the implementation that turns it green. This is a discipline — **not** a synonym for the noun `contract`.
provenance: Per the v1→v2 term map, v1's "story is a contract" / red-green reads as this principle, not the noun `contract`.

title: mock-UAT seam
description: No mocks within an organism: capability integration tests and the story UAT both run against real in-story collaborators.
body:
**In one line.** The rule that there are no mocks within an organism — capability integration tests and the story UAT both run against real in-story collaborators — with the sole stubbable boundary being the declared cross-story boundary.

## What it is

**No mocks within an organism**: capability integration tests and the story UAT both run against real in-story collaborators. The one stubbable boundary is the declared cross-story **boundary** — a story's UAT may run against a stubbed / contract-tested version of an upstream story's interface (like acceptance-testing a frontend against a stubbed database). Isolated unit (contract) tests still stub freely.

## What it is not

This is isolation, not theatre — stubbing at the declared cross-story seam is legitimate, whereas faking collaborators inside the organism is a structural defect.

title: defects-amend-the-owning-story
description: A defect amends the capability whose contract it violates (reverting it to building), rather than spawning a new unit.
body:
**The principle.** When a defect violates a capability's contract, amend the owning capability (reverting it to `building`) rather than spawning a new unit.

## Why

A defect could be filed as a brand-new unit, fragmenting ownership of a behaviour across the unit that owns it and the unit that records its bug.

## How to apply

Route the defect to the capability whose contract it violates; revert that capability to `building` and fix it under its existing contract. You trade a new unit's clean slate for a single accountable owner per behaviour and an intact contract/evidence chain on the original capability.

title: fail-closed-on-dirty-tree
description: A command that writes attestable evidence refuses to run on a dirty working tree (writes nothing, non-zero exit code).
body:
**The principle.** A command that writes attestable evidence must not run on a dirty working tree — it writes nothing and exits non-zero.

## Why

Evidence attributed to a clean commit could silently include uncommitted changes, corrupting the proof/evidence chain that promotion depends on.

## How to apply

Have such a command check working-tree cleanliness before doing any evidence-writing work and abort on a dirty tree — the load-bearing guarantee is that it writes nothing (no signing row, no event), with a non-zero exit code. Make it a code-path guard, not a warning, so the boundary is non-bypassable in practice. (Reserving a distinct exit code for the dirty-tree refusal — rather than the generic non-zero every refusal returns today — is a cheap future hardening, not a current guarantee.)

title: verification-wins
description: The stance that binding to external truth via tests + on-disk evidence overrides LLM memory consolidation / recency ("recency-wins").
body:
**The principle.** Binding to external truth via tests + on-disk evidence **overrides** LLM memory consolidation / recency ("recency-wins").

## Why

A confident-but-stale model recollection can override what the tests and evidence actually show. v2 rejects Dreams-style memory reconciliation in favour of a commit/event-bound evidence chain.

## How to apply

When the model's memory and the evidence chain disagree, the evidence wins — trust the tests + on-disk record over recency.
provenance: The learning loop's v2 home is open (`open-questions.md` §5).

title: claim
description: A typed write-ownership record (a row/event in the one shared event store) naming what a node intends to write, checked under a serializable/unique constraint at node-schedule time;
body:
**In one line.** A typed write-ownership record in the one shared event store, naming what a node intends to write and checked under a serializable/unique constraint at node-schedule time — a conflict is a hard refusal, never a warning.

## What it is

A typed **write-ownership** record (a row/event in the one shared event store) naming what a node intends to write, checked under a serializable/unique constraint at **node-schedule time**; a conflict is a **hard refusal** (a `claim-conflict-refused` event), never a warning.

Granularity, the conflict-resolution ceremony, and whether code *edits* still use a git branch/worktree per node are open (`open-questions.md` §3).

## What it is not

Not v1's per-worktree `session_claims` table (Agentic ADR-0022): the v2 successor now lives in the single shared Postgres store (ADR-0009).
provenance: See `open-questions.md` §3.

title: write-ownership
description: The single vocabulary for what surface/unit a node claims the right to write, used by the claim / conflict-detection layer.
body:
**In one line.** The single vocabulary for what surface/unit a node claims the right to write, used by the claim / conflict-detection layer.

## What it is

The single vocabulary for *what surface/unit* a node claims the right to write, used by the claim / conflict-detection layer. It unifies v1's scattered terms (`declared_scope` vs per-agent `does_not_touch`) into one concept (ADR-0009); the exact shape (node-scoped vs file-glob) is open (`open-questions.md` §3).

## What it is not

Not v1's scattered, split vocabulary (`declared_scope` and per-agent `does_not_touch`), which this single concept replaces.
provenance: See `open-questions.md` §3.

title: orchestrator
description: The thin custom TypeScript layer (packages/orchestrator) over plain Postgres (DBOS deferred, ADR-0019): owns the story-DAG, the scheduler, and the event store, and is the only module that drives `packages/agent` (the owned loop).
body:
**In one line.** The thin custom TypeScript layer (`packages/orchestrator`) over plain Postgres (DBOS deferred, ADR-0019) that owns the story-DAG, the scheduler, and the event store, and is the only module that drives `packages/agent` (the owned loop).

## What it is

The thin custom TypeScript layer (`packages/orchestrator`) over plain Postgres — DBOS is deferred (ADR-0019), so this is a plain typed Postgres connection (ADR-0001): it owns the story-DAG, the scheduler, and the event store, and is the **only** module that drives `packages/agent` (the owned loop). It is the code-sequenced **spine** and the sole **fan-out** point — it schedules nodes; owned-loop nodes never schedule child nodes.

## What it is not

Distinct from an owned-loop session, which owns work *inside* a node (ADR-0004, ADR-0005, ADR-0011).
provenance: DBOS deferred by ADR-0019.

title: spine
description: The code-sequenced control-flow layer (the orchestrator over plain Postgres, DBOS deferred — ADR-0019) that owns closed, deterministic routing: the order steps run in, when a loop iterates, which branch is taken.
body:
**In one line.** The code-sequenced control-flow layer (the orchestrator over plain Postgres, DBOS deferred — ADR-0019) that owns closed, deterministic routing — the order steps run in, when a loop iterates, which branch is taken.

## What it is

The code-sequenced control-flow layer (the orchestrator over plain Postgres — DBOS is deferred, ADR-0019) that owns **closed, deterministic routing**: the order steps run in, when a loop iterates, which branch is taken. The discriminator (carried verbatim from Agentic ADR-0026): *if a for-loop or a match could express the routing, the spine owns it; if the routing needs the model to decide what comes next, the leaf (owned-loop node) owns it.*

## What it is not

Not the **leaf** — the leaf runtime the spine delegates to (live: the Claude Agent SDK; offline/fallback: the owned loop — ADR-0030).
provenance: Carried verbatim from Agentic ADR-0026; DBOS deferred by ADR-0019; leaf runtime split live/offline by ADR-0030.

title: leaf step / leaf judgment
description: A single step in a code-sequenced cascade whose work is owned by the leaf runtime (what to write, how to satisfy a contract) rather than by the spine — the control-flow sense of "leaf"…
body:
**In one line.** A single step in a code-sequenced cascade whose work is owned by the leaf runtime (what to write, how to satisfy a contract) rather than by the spine — the control-flow sense of "leaf".

## What it is

A single step in a code-sequenced cascade whose work is owned by the **leaf runtime** (what to write, how to satisfy a contract) rather than by the spine — the **control-flow** sense of "leaf" (ADR-0005, ADR-0011). The leaf runs behind the executor seam: live, the Claude Agent SDK; offline/fallback, the owned loop (ADR-0030).

## What it is not

Distinct from **contract**, the *leaf tier* of the work hierarchy (ADR-0002); the two senses of "leaf" must not be conflated.

title: agent package
description: The project-owned thin wrapper (`packages/agent`) and typed-event parser over the leaf surface (prompt / steer / follow-up + lifecycle events + edit-tool diffs).
body:
**In one line.** The project-owned thin wrapper (`packages/agent`) and typed-event parser over the leaf surface (`prompt` / `steer` / `follow-up` + lifecycle events + `edit`-tool diffs).

## What it is

The project-owned **thin wrapper** (`packages/agent`) and typed-event parser over the leaf surface (`prompt` / `steer` / `follow-up` + lifecycle events + `edit`-tool diffs). The **sole** surface through which a leaf runtime is invoked and the **only** place a model runtime is imported. It hosts both executors behind the `PhaseAuthor` seam: the owned loop on the Messages API (offline/deterministic + pivot-out fallback, ADR-0011) and the Claude Agent SDK leaf (`ClaudeAgentAuthor` — the live runtime, ADR-0030); it normalizes the stream into the typed events the event store renders, and exposes nothing model-shaped upward.

## What it is not

No third-party agent framework other than the single-import-site Agent SDK sits in this role; `packages/storage-protocol` and `apps/studio` never parse the leaf's stream directly (ADR-0004, ADR-0006, ADR-0011, ADR-0030).
provenance: Carries v1's own-a-thin-wrapper-over-the-agent-runtime principle (Agentic ADR-0008/0026).

title: trunk

## What it is not

Not v1's trunk, which it supersedes — v1's auto-merged on green and tolerated broken intermediate states under an eventual-consistency posture.

title: Template — open-question
description: Fillable scaffold for a new open-question artifact (an unresolved decision to settle).
body:
**Why this matters.** _What breaks, or what job is blocked, if this stays unsettled — one sentence a newcomer (or an agent without the repo loaded) understands, before any identifier or ADR number._

## The question

_The decision to settle, in one sentence._

## Context

_Why it is open now — the forces and constraints, and what is blocked until it lands. Gloss every internal term, code identifier, and ADR number on first use._

## Diagram

_A picture when the subject is a structure, flow, or state machine — a ```mermaid fenced block (rendered as an SVG in the studio, ADR-0096) or an ASCII box/flow diagram in a fenced code block. Omit for a pure value/policy choice._

## Options

_The candidate answers, each with its trade-off (name both sides — A vs B)._

## Recommendation

_The proposed answer and why — explicitly non-binding until the owner decides._

title: lifecycle status
description: A unit's status, drawn from six states — proposed, building, healthy, unhealthy, mapped, retired — that track it from authoring to proven to pruned.
body:
**In one line.** A unit's status, drawn from six states — proposed, building, healthy, unhealthy, mapped, retired — that track it from authoring through proven to pruned.

## What it is

Status lives on every tier (story / capability / contract) and is drawn from six states:

- **proposed** — authored but not yet selected for implementation; the initial state.

- **building** — selected and under active implementation (v1: `under_construction`); written at pickup as the first commit, before any code edits.

- **healthy** — proven: the unit reached `healthy` through its tier's proof mode at HEAD — a story by a UAT pass over fresh green capabilities, a capability by integration tests over fresh green contracts, a contract by its isolated unit test (or, where neither honest test exists, operator-attested) (ADR-0010).

- **unhealthy** — a once-healthy capability that has drifted (a contract test now fails, owned files changed, or the proof no longer matches HEAD); **computed** from evidence, never written to disk.

- **mapped** — brownfield: the authored status of a capability mapped in from an existing target-repo suite, awaiting **adoption** — a pre-proof authored state like `proposed`, and distinct from `healthy` the same way. Its go-green path is *adopt*: the spine observes the existing suite green at a clean HEAD and signs an `adopted` verdict, transitioning it to `healthy` (ADR-0085 / ADR-0097). Adopt (observe) and drive (a red→green) are **peer best-efforts** to bring code under the TDD umbrella; the `adopted` basis records *how* a green was established — value-neutral provenance that ranks nothing; an `adopted` `healthy` is a full peer of a driven one (ADR-0105).

- **retired** — terminal off-tree state: pruned from the active tree; may carry `retired_reason` (prose) and `superseded_by` (an edge to its replacement).

## What it is not

`healthy` is not `mapped` and not `unhealthy`. `mapped` is the authored brownfield status a capability sits in *before* adoption — the brownfield counterpart of `proposed`; adoption observes its existing suite green and signs an `adopted` verdict to reach `healthy`, a full peer of a driven green (ADR-0085 / ADR-0105). `unhealthy` is a once-healthy unit that has since drifted, computed from evidence and never written to disk.
provenance: The `mapped` exit is settled — *adopt* (observe-and-sign → an `adopted` verdict; ADR-0085 / ADR-0097), reframed as value-neutral provenance by ADR-0105. Remaining follow-on: fixture/model version-pinning for an external target repo (`open-questions.md` §2).

title: unit fields
description: The four core fields a unit carries — outcome, guidance, title, id — covering its value statement, rebuild context, human label, and unique identifier.
body:
**In one line.** The four core fields a unit carries — outcome, guidance, title, id — covering its value statement, rebuild context, human label, and unique identifier.

## What it is

The four core fields a unit carries:

- **outcome** — a capability's plain-English, single-sentence value statement, with no conjunctions (if it needs them, split the unit instead).

- **guidance** — non-obvious technical context needed to rebuild a unit; only what an agent could not derive from outcome + proof.

- **title** — a short human label for a unit; not load-bearing for proof.

- **id** — a unit's unique identifier. v2 must allocate these **conflict-free across concurrent sessions** — a stated goal. An earlier DBOS spike showed durable, collision-free workflow IDs as one candidate mechanism, but DBOS is deferred (ADR-0019), so that mechanism is reference, not the adopted id-allocation path.

title: Cloud SQL access · Node connector · keyless IAM
description: How storytree reaches its Postgres runtime store: the Cloud SQL Node connector with IAM database auth via ambient ADC. Agent sessions need no per-session OAuth and no key files.
body:
**The choice.** Reach the Cloud SQL Postgres via the Node connector with keyless IAM database authentication — agent sessions authenticate from ambient Application Default Credentials, never a per-session OAuth or a key file.

## What it is

The `@google-cloud/cloud-sql-connector` Node connector (paired with `pg`, in `packages/store`) mints short-lived IAM tokens as the database password over a TLS socket — no password is stored. Identity comes from the operator’s Application Default Credentials already established on the dev host (plus a dedicated service account for headless/impersonated runs). The IAM database user is the operator email (`STORYTREE_DB_USER`), the database is `storytree`, and the instance connection name is `storytree-498613:australia-southeast1:storytree-pg`.

## Why this

No secrets in the repo and no per-session interactive login, so agents can run migrations and queries headlessly; it matches the stop/start cost posture (ADR-0015 §5) and keeps the browser/credential-free boundary (the studio never holds DB creds). A session should ASSUME keyless credentials are present and verify with `gcloud auth application-default print-access-token`, not assume it is unauthenticated.

## Constraints

The instance is STOPPED by default and bills only while up — burst with `pnpm db:up` → work → `pnpm db:down`. Auto-stop is **idle-aware** (ADR-0015 §5, `infra/idle-stop.tf`): a Cloud Function stops the instance only after ~60 min with zero DB connections, so it never stops a live session (a connection held by a session / the Auth Proxy resets the idle timer); a once-a-day blunt cron is the cost floor behind it. So a long idle gap will stop the DB — re-`db:up` if a query fails to connect. IAM users are bare roles, so a ONE-TIME privilege bootstrap (`infra/bootstrap-grants.sql`, applied as the throwaway-password `postgres` user via `packages/store/scripts/apply-grants.mjs`) granted the IAM user `CREATE` on the database; this is idempotent and already applied. The connector deps live in `packages/store`; DBOS is deferred (ADR-0019), so this is a plain typed Postgres connection.
provenance: Validated 2026-06-08: 73 units migrated keyless, then the instance stopped.

title: Doc-vs-implementation precedence
description: Implementation is ground truth; when a doc claim and the code disagree, the gap itself is the load-bearing surface — surface it, do not build on the doc claim.
body:
**The principle.** Implementation is ground truth and doc text is a hypothesis about it; when a finding shows a doc claim (ADR, glossary, guideline, spec) disagrees with the code, the gap itself is the load-bearing surface, not metadata to a move that took the doc at face value.

## Why

It is easy to author the next move — 'extend the code so the doc holds', 'tighten the invariant' — on top of a doc claim a recent finding has already shown false. That move is built on a phantom premise. The honest first question is whether the doc needs correcting to match the code (most common) or the code needs extending to make the doc's claim hold (rarer, an operator call).

## How to apply

Discriminator: does a recent investigation name a doc-vs-code gap, AND does your pending move cite that same doc as load-bearing? Yes on both → reshape any framing that takes the claim at face value and surface the gap to the operator (the doc, its exact claim, the observed behaviour, one question: correct the doc or extend the code?) without pre-deciding. Three resolutions: correct the doc (most common; the downstream move usually dissolves), route code-extension as its own bounded unit (operator-directed), or defer.
provenance: Composes with the `assess-tradeoffs-by-naming-both-sides` pattern (that governs how a surfaced tradeoff is framed; this governs whether the surface should exist). Imported from v1 `assets/guidelines/doc-vs-implementation-precedence.yml`.

title: Dogfood: fix the source
description: When your own tooling blocks you, fix the tool at its source rather than papering over it with a one-off workaround.
body:
**The principle.** When tooling we own blocks progress, fix the tool or its source; do not mask the symptom with a one-off workaround that leaves the root cause live to resurface.

## Why

storytree is built with the very kind of agent-driven system it builds (orchestrator, spine, owned loop, studio), so a defect in our own tooling is a first-class bug, not an environmental annoyance. A null guard, hardcoded value, swallowed exception, or shell fallback gets you unblocked now but hides a real defect; the problem goes quiet and re-emerges later, harder to diagnose under a layer of masking, and the next session inherits the same wall.

## How to apply

Reproduce and trace to the earliest failing signal; validate that a test genuinely captures the failure before changing production code; fix the root cause where it lives so the bad state cannot occur (not a guard that hides it after the fact); prefer making one behaviour simply correct over adding a flag. Anti-patterns: a catch-all that does not handle the error, hardcoding a value to silence a dynamic failure, a narrow edge-case handler for a problem that should be fixed generically, and falling back to a workaround tool without recording that the primary one failed.
