(3) Draw the structure — if the subject is a structure, flow, or state machine, include a diagram, not only prose. This is the structural cure `guidance-quality` argues for — a template field or a slot, not a "please write clearly" exhortation; the `open-question` template operationalises check 1 via its required `stakes` lead and check 3 via its `## Diagram` slot.

title: A driven test must not leak an OS handle
description: A test authored for a `--real` build-tests drive must release every OS resource it opens (socket, handle, timer, child) before it returns — the spine now SIGKILLs a hung observation (a fail-closed RED, not an infinite wedge), but a passing test that leaks still burns the full timeout and reads as a slow false-red.
body:
**The principle.** A test authored for a `--real` red→green drive must close every OS resource it opens — socket, file handle, timer, child process — before it returns, and restore any mutated process state in a `finally`. A leaked handle keeps the test process alive after the assertions finish; the spine's observation now carries a wall-clock timeout that SIGKILLs the survivor (`DEFAULT_PROOF_TIMEOUT_MS`, ~10 min) and records a fail-closed RED, so a leak no longer wedges the drive — but a genuinely-passing test that leaks burns the full budget and is reported as a slow false-red.

## Why

The spine observes red/green by spawning the proof command and reading its exit code (`runShellCommand` → `execFile`, `packages/orchestrator/src/shell-test-executor.ts`), and that spawn now carries a `timeout` (`cmd.timeoutMs ?? DEFAULT_PROOF_TIMEOUT_MS`, ~10 min) and `killSignal: "SIGKILL"`, so a child that outruns its budget is killed and surfaces as a fail-closed RED instead of running forever. The proof command is the package suite, run WITHOUT `--test-force-exit`, so the runner does not force the process down once the tests pass: a single open handle keeps it alive until that timeout fires — so a leak no longer stalls the gate forever, but a test whose assertions all PASSED is killed mid-leak and recorded as a RED (a slow false-red): the budget is burned and nothing actually failed. The residual reason to follow the rule is a fast, honest green, not a backstop that no longer exists. This is most acute in a build-tests gate over connection/DB/IO code, where the natural way to exercise the red still opens a real resource: a fail-closed `createPool` guard, for example, may build a real connector (~6s with ambient ADC) on the not-yet-guarded path before the assertion runs (verified 2026-06-25 driving `library#gate-5`).

## How to apply

On the path where the resource IS opened (typically the pre-fix RED path, before the guard exists), capture the handle and close it (`closePool(pool, connector)`, `server.close()`, `clearTimeout`, `child.kill`) BEFORE asserting the failure — never assert first and leak the handle on the throw. Restore any env var or global the test mutated (e.g. a `delete process.env.X` used to force the no-principal case) in a `finally`, so a sibling test is not poisoned. Keep an offline fail-closed assertion offline: it should throw before opening anything, so the GREEN path (post-fix) opens nothing and the suite exits on its own. If a live leg is genuinely needed, gate it behind the live-DB env var (`STORYTREE_DB_LIVE`) the spine does not set, so it can neither open a connection nor hang CONFIRM_GREEN in the gated drive. The deletion test still applies: the close/restore is plumbing, not behaviour — it must not weaken what the test pins.
provenance: Graduated 2026-06-25 (ADR-0095) from the durable inner-loop gotcha surfaced while driving `library#gate-5` (the R1 build-tests gate over the keyless `connection.ts` fail-closed contract) — encoded as the 'Close-on-resolve' note in `stories/library/event-sourced-store-seam.md`'s `## Guidance`. Reconciled 2026-06-25 after the spine-hardening landed (`runShellCommand` gained `DEFAULT_PROOF_TIMEOUT_MS` + `killSignal: "SIGKILL"`): the original 'infinite wedge / no backstop' stake is overtaken. It STAYS a principle, not a guardrail — the spine now BOUNDS the worst case (a hung proof is killed → fail-closed RED) but does not ENFORCE the rule (a leaking-but-passing test still burns the full timeout and false-reds), so it lives on the test-author leaf's reading — the enforcer test (guidance-authoring review, 2026-06-15) routes a rule with no live enforcer to `principle`. Consumers: red-builder.

title: The owner-fork bar: escalate ownership, not uncertainty
description: An open-question is earned only by a decision that is the owner's to make — irreversible, outward-facing, or value-laden and unsettleable from the corpus; a reversible, internal call with a defensible engineering answer is decided and recorded, never parked as an owner fork.
body:
**The principle.** Raise an owner-facing fork (an `open-question`) only when the DECISION ITSELF is the owner's to make — it is irreversible, outward-facing, or value-laden and unsettleable from the existing decision log — never merely because the agent is unsure; a reversible, internal call with a defensible engineering answer is decided and recorded (an ADR if it outlives its unit, else the unit's own guidance), not parked as an OQ.

## Why

Owner adjudication is the system's scarcest channel, and a misrouted fork spends it twice: the owner must first reverse-engineer the engineering context the agent already had, then make a call the agent was equipped to make — so the fork cannot be answered in seconds, sits unadjudicated, and the unit stalls behind it. The observed failure is exactly this: forks like "which proof command" or "tighten this glob predicate" reached the owner already carrying an engineering lean, because uncertainty — not ownership — was used as the trigger. The opposite error is as costly: deciding an irreversible or value-laden call unilaterally removes the human from the loop they own (`human-owns-the-outer-loop`). The asymmetry favours deciding-and-recording: a decidable engineering call mis-routed to the owner wastes the one resource that does not scale, whereas the same call decided by a competent agent costs only a moment of judgement and leaves a durable record.

## How to apply

Ask of any candidate fork: is the DECISION the owner's? It earns an `open-question` iff at least one holds — (1) IRREVERSIBLE or high-blast-radius (destroys history, migrates or deletes data, lands on the product trunk, or is otherwise costly to undo); (2) OUTWARD-FACING (changes what an external party sees or depends on — the public site, users, a deploy, an email); (3) VALUE-LADEN and UNSETTLEABLE FROM THE CORPUS (the right answer turns on owner priorities, product direction, taste, or risk appetite, not on an engineering tradeoff the decision log already settles). If none hold, it is your call: pick the defensible option, name both sides of the tradeoff (`assess-tradeoffs-by-naming-both-sides`), RECORD it — an ADR if the decision would outlive the unit that prompted it (`survival-test-for-adrs`), otherwise the unit's own guidance — and move on. Tells that a fork is already below the bar: it carries a stated "lean", its rationale is purely engineering, or it cannot be put to the owner in one plain sentence without an identifier or ADR number. The discriminator is OWNERSHIP, not ADR-worthiness: a cross-cutting but purely-engineering standard is an ADR you author yourself (ADR-0084), not an owner fork.
provenance: Authored 2026-06-22 at the owner's steer, after two live OQs (`oq-edit-existing-refinements`, `oq-gate-as-proof-hygiene`) reached the owner reading like engineering-hygiene calls (a glob predicate, a proof-command scope) the owner could not adjudicate in seconds — a ROUTING gap, not a wording one. States once the bar that was previously restated, partially, across the `escalation` fields of session-orchestrator, guidance-curator, story-author, librarian-curator, and graduation-synthesist (reference-don't-restate; two-consumer-extraction — 5 current consumers). The sibling of `survival-test-for-adrs` (which routes a decision between an ADR and a unit's guidance) for the owner-vs-engineer axis; complements the `human-owns-the-outer-loop` guardrail (which fixes WHO performs an outer-loop transition) by fixing WHAT rises to one. Consumers: session-orchestrator (wired into rules); recommended for the OQ-authoring escalation paths (guidance-curator, graduation-synthesist).

title: De-noising promotes a signal, never drops it
description: When a dense observability surface is de-noised, every signal is kept as a low-salience rendering — a stamp, a thin line, a badge — never deleted; de-noise lowers the volume of a signal, it does not hide it.
body:
**The principle.** When you de-noise a crowded view, demote each crowding element to a low-salience rendering — never remove it. The boundary is sharp: lowering a real coupling's salience (a thin line, a small per-thing stamp) is de-noise; making it un-recoverable from the picture is a drop, and a drop is information loss. If a thing was true enough to draw, it stays drawn at lower volume.

## Why

The observability surface exists to expose couplings the operator must see and judge — that is its whole job (`observability-first`: the system surfaces, the operator decides). A de-noise that DROPS an edge or flattens distinct edges into one generic badge silently erases exactly the signal the surface was built to carry, and the loss is invisible: the picture looks clean precisely because the thing you needed is gone. This is the trap ADR-0088's generic consumer-only bookshelf stamp fell into — it kept that an island used SOME shared island but lost WHICH one and the DIRECTION, a lossy near-drop that hid the `studio → cli` coupling the boundary system exists to surface (ADR-0102 Context §1). The cost is paid twice: a dropped signal cannot be judged, and a flattened one looks benign, so an architecturally important relationship reads as routine.

## How to apply

Before removing anything to reduce clutter, ask: is what I am removing a SIGNAL (a real coupling, a real state) or pure decoration? A signal must be PROMOTED, not dropped — re-rendered at lower salience but still recoverable: ADR-0074 §1 ('de-noise visually … never by dropping edges') is the dependency-graph instance; ADR-0102 generalises it to ANY visual de-noise — a shared island promotes each incident edge to a per-island icon stamp (the edge kept as a badge, placement carrying its direction), rather than the road vanishing. Prefer a de-noise that carries the SAME information at lower volume (identity + direction preserved) over one that summarises it away (a generic 'uses a shared thing' badge drops which and whither). Reach for an outright drop only for genuine decoration that carries no signal. This is the world-map application of `signal-and-noise` (cut noise, keep signal): here the real coupling is the signal, the low-salience stamp is the de-noise. Whether a surfaced coupling is WRONG stays a human read, not a render-time verdict — promotion keeps it visible so the operator can judge it (`observability-first`); it does not flag it.
provenance: Graduated 2026-06-25 (ADR-0095 / the ADR-0102 build's librarian pass) — ADR-0102 named this durable rule ('de-noising promotes an edge, never drops it') but HELD authoring it until a second consumer existed; the per-island-icon-stamp build is that second consumer (the first being ADR-0074 §1's dependency-graph form). A distinct principle, not a refinement of `signal-and-noise`: it carries a rule that one does not — promote-not-drop, de-noise-never-hides-signal — and applies to the observability render, not to agent-readable guidance. ADR-0102 §References confirms the angle is novel ('No existing artifact covers the de-noise-never-hides-signal rule'). Generalises ADR-0074 §1 from the dependency graph to any visual de-noise; cross-links `signal-and-noise` (parent: cut noise, keep signal) and `observability-first` (sibling: the surface exposes, the operator judges).

title: Rename: 'UAT tests' → 'UAT test criteria' (de-overload 'tests')
description: A deferred vocabulary rename: the story-level acceptance items (ADR-0044's per-test 'UAT tests' under `## Story UAT`) become **UAT test criteria**, so 'tests' stops colliding with `node:test` unit tests. node:test, contracts, and the stored `#uat-<n>` ids stay unchanged. Parked until a quiet, no-active-sessions window.
body:
**The proposal.** Rename the story-level acceptance items — ADR-0044's per-test 'UAT tests', parsed from each story's `## Story UAT` section into `<story>#uat-<n>` — to **UAT test criteria** (singular: a **UAT test criterion**), so the word **tests** stops colliding with `node:test` unit tests. The decision is made; only the execution is deferred to a quiet window.

## Motivation

The single word *tests* is overloaded across storytree, and a reader can't always tell which sense is meant. It names at least four different things: (a) `node:test` UNIT tests in `*.test.ts` — the real, runnable, automated suites (`pnpm -r test`); (b) per-capability **contracts**, each defined as 'one automated, isolated unit test', the leaf of the proof ladder; (c) the story-level **acceptance walkthrough** items under `## Story UAT`, which ADR-0044 calls 'UAT tests' (`<story>#uat-<n>`, the attestation surface); and (d) the informal 'blind test / dogfood test'. The sharpest collision is in the studio, which labels the acceptance list **'UAT tests'** in a panel sitting right beside the runnable suites — and in prose where 'the UAT tests' could mean either a thing you *run* or a thing you *satisfy*. Naming the story-level acceptance items **UAT test criteria** disambiguates the one tier that most invites confusion: a *criterion* is a condition to be satisfied or attested, not a suite to be executed — which is exactly what an ADR-0044 acceptance item is (it is attested by a human or a machine, never 'run' as a `node:test`). The genuinely-runnable senses (a, b) keep the word *test*. The cost of leaving it: every new story author re-meets the 'which test is this?' ambiguity, and the UI keeps mixing acceptance criteria with executable suites.

## The change

Before → after (name the old and new term exactly):

**Vocabulary (the heart of the rename):**
- One acceptance item — today **a UAT test** (ADR-0044) → **a UAT test criterion**.
- The list of them — today **UAT tests** → **UAT test criteria**.
- The framing stays a *walkthrough*: the `## Story UAT` section is still the story's user-acceptance walkthrough (the `uat` library definition — 'user-acceptance walkthrough' — is UNCHANGED); only the per-item noun moves from 'UAT test' to 'UAT test criterion'.

**Section heading (a SUB-DECISION — confirm the exact wording with the owner at execution, like the precedent's tree-vs-capability grain):**
- RECOMMENDED: `## Story UAT` → `## UAT Test Criteria`, for consistency with the new noun. This is the riskiest single change because the heading is the parser anchor `STORY_UAT_HEADING` (`packages/core/src/uat-tests.ts`) — it MUST move in lockstep with that regex, transitionally accepting BOTH headings so no story's criteria parse to `[]` mid-migration.
- ALTERNATIVE: keep `## Story UAT` and change only the item vocabulary + identifiers — a clean partial if the heading question can't be settled.

**Code identifiers (`packages/core` + consumers):**
- module `packages/core/src/uat-tests.ts` → `uat-test-criteria.ts`
- type `UatTest` → `UatTestCriterion`
- field `uatTests` (on `NodeSpec`) → `uatTestCriteria`
- fn `parseUatTests(...)` → `parseUatTestCriteria(...)`
- fn `uatTestId(...)` → `uatTestCriterionId(...)` (NB: its OUTPUT string scheme `<story>#uat-<n>` does NOT change — see Scope)
- OPTIONAL/low-value: the per-criterion witness enum `UatTestWitness` / `UAT_TEST_WITNESSES` → `*Criterion*` for consistency; can be deferred (it names a *witness*, not a 'test').

**User-facing labels:**
- studio panel heading `UAT tests ({n})` (`apps/studio/src/components/TreeView.tsx`) → `UAT test criteria ({n})`; CSS `.uat-tests` / `.uat-test-*` and the `uatTestsForStory` / `/api/attestations` field names → `uatTestCriteria*`.
- `storytree tree`'s `UAT tests:` block label (`packages/cli/src/tree.ts`) → `UAT test criteria:`.

**JUDGEMENT CALL — surfaced, not guessed (per the brief):** the per-capability **contracts** (b) STAY 'contracts'. This is a category decision, not a style one: a contract is defined as 'one automated, isolated unit test', and ADR-0010 places UAT at the STORY level only (capabilities get integration tests, contracts get unit tests, NOT UATs). Calling a contract a 'UAT test criterion' would be a category error, so contracts keep their already-distinct, well-chosen name. Likewise `node:test` unit tests (a) stay 'tests' (they are genuine tests) and the informal 'blind/dogfood test' (d) stays (colloquial, not a structured term).

## Scope

**Surfaces / files / identifiers touched:**
- `packages/core`: `src/uat-tests.ts` (the `UatTest` schema, `parseUatTests`, `uatTestId`, and the `STORY_UAT_HEADING` regex), `src/uat-tests.test.ts`, and the `@storytree/core` barrel `src/index.ts` re-exports.
- `packages/orchestrator`: `src/node-spec.ts` (imports `parseUatTests` + `UatTest`; the `NodeSpec.uatTests` field at `:82`,`:129`); `src/story-build.test.ts` fixtures (`uatTests: []`).
- `packages/cli`: `src/tree.ts` (the `UAT tests:` render block + `UatTest` import at `:13`,`:161`,`:237-241`); `src/attest.ts` (help/next prose says 'per-UAT-test'); fixtures in `src/oq-gate.test.ts`, `src/gate-as-proof.test.ts`.
- `apps/studio`: `src/components/TreeView.tsx` ('UAT tests' panel heading + `.uat-test-*`), `src/types.ts` (the `UatTest` type + GET `/api/attestations` response shape), `src/index.css` (`.uat-tests` / `.uat-test-cell` / `.uat-test-title` / `.uat-test-who`), `src/api.ts`, `server/apiRouter.ts` (`uatTestsForStory`, the `/api/attestations` endpoint), `server/libraryBackend.ts`.
- `stories/**`: the `## Story UAT` heading + 'UAT test' prose in the 11 story files that carry the section (uat-attestation, studio, studio-members ×3, studio-cloud, notice-board, library, feedback-graduation, drive-machinery, ci-cd). NOTE: `stories/drive-machinery/story.md` was just extended (2026-06-15) with Story UAT criterion 7 in the CURRENT vocabulary — a prime example of exactly the prose this targets.
- `docs/`: ADR-0044 prose (and its ADR-0040 cross-refs), `docs/glossary.md` if it carries the term, and the `uat` library-definition body where it says 'UAT tests'.
- Library: the `uat` definition artifact (live store + `knowledge.json` seed) and this proposal artifact.

**STORED DATA — the riskiest part, explicitly a NON-GOAL:** the attestation join key `<story>#uat-<n>` (the output of `uatTestId`) and the `events.attestation.test_id` column are STORED, append-only, SIGNED ids. Renaming the scheme or the column would ORPHAN every recorded attestation (prod already carries a synthetic `_deploy-verify#uat-1` row; ADR-0044) and force a migration of a signed log. LEAVE BOTH UNCHANGED — they are opaque stable keys; the 'uat' in them does not collide with 'test', and renaming them buys nothing against real data-loss risk. This is the single most important non-goal.

**Other explicit NON-GOALS (the word 'test' stays):** `node:test` unit tests, `*.test.ts`, `pnpm -r test`, the gate (a — genuine tests); the per-capability **contracts** (b — a category error to rename, see The change); ADR-0040's story-level witness vocabulary (`uat_witness` frontmatter, `UatWitness`, `effectiveUatWitness` — it names who *witnessed*, not the criteria list); the npm scope `@storytree/*`, the `storytree` CLI binary, and the DB schema beyond the `test_id` column comment.

## Migration plan

Run as ONE dedicated session, core-first, gating green at each step:

1. **Record the decision.** Allocate + land an ADR (`pnpm storytree adr new --title "..." --pg`) capturing the rename, the contracts-stay rationale (ADR-0010 category argument), and the stored-id non-goal — so the rename has a deciding record.
2. **Pin the heading wording with the owner** (`## Story UAT` → `## UAT Test Criteria`, or keep `## Story UAT`). This is the one term that must be settled before any story file is touched.
3. **`packages/core` first.** Rename `uat-tests.ts` → `uat-test-criteria.ts`; `UatTest`→`UatTestCriterion`, `uatTests`→`uatTestCriteria`, `parseUatTests`→`parseUatTestCriteria`, `uatTestId`→`uatTestCriterionId`. If the heading changes, WIDEN `STORY_UAT_HEADING` to a transitional alternation accepting BOTH old and new headings (so nothing parses to `[]` mid-flight). Update `src/index.ts` re-exports + the test file. Verify: `pnpm --filter @storytree/core test` green.
4. **Sweep consumers in one pass.** `packages/orchestrator/src/node-spec.ts`, `packages/cli/src/tree.ts` + `attest.ts`, and all fixtures (`uatTests: []` → `uatTestCriteria: []`). Verify: `pnpm -r typecheck && pnpm -r test` green. (Watch the pinned id-list tests — update them in the same commit, per the real-build-typecheck-wall lesson.)
5. **Studio.** Rename the panel heading + `.uat-test-*` classes + `uatTestsForStory` + the `/api/attestations` field names + `src/types.ts`. Verify: `pnpm --filter studio build` + `pnpm --filter studio test` green.
6. **Story files.** Rename the `## Story UAT` headings (if step 2 said so) + 'UAT test' prose across the 11 story files; re-run `parseUatTestCriteria` per story (e.g. `storytree tree <story>`) to confirm the SAME criteria still parse with UNCHANGED ids. Then, if the regex was widened in step 3, tighten it to the new heading only.
7. **Library.** `storytree library artifact edit uat --set ... --pg` to fix 'UAT tests' → 'UAT test criteria' in the `uat` definition body; migrate the `knowledge.json` seed + regenerate the derived view (`npx tsx apps/studio/data/build-corpus.mjs`); retire or keep this proposal.
8. **Prose sweep.** ADR-0044, CLAUDE.md, README, docs for 'UAT test(s)' → 'UAT test criterion/criteria' at the acceptance grain.
9. **Full gate + smoke.** `pnpm gate` green; studio smoke (the 'UAT test criteria' panel renders, attestation ids unchanged).

## Readiness

Run in a QUIET window: `storytree noticeboard` shows no other active sessions, and NO open PR touches `packages/core`, `apps/studio`, or `stories/**` — a sweep this wide conflicts with any parallel branch, and story files are actively edited (criterion 7 just landed on drive-machinery). Kick off only from a green `pnpm gate` on `main`. Bring the DB up for the `uat`-definition edit (step 7) but otherwise leave it idle. The owner must have confirmed the `## Story UAT` heading wording (step 2) first. Because it spans core + orchestrator + cli + studio + stories + docs + the library in one coherent sweep, give it ONE dedicated session, not interleaved with feature work. A session knows it is time when: no conflicting PRs are open, the gate is green on `main`, and the heading wording is settled.

## Risks

**The parser is the sharp edge.** If a `## Story UAT` heading is renamed but `STORY_UAT_HEADING` is not widened in lockstep, `parseUatTestCriteria` silently returns `[]` — a story's criteria AND their whole attestation surface vanish with no error. Mitigation: do `packages/core` first with a transitional dual-accept regex; verify every migrated story re-parses to the SAME criterion ids before tightening it.
**Never touch the stored ids.** Renaming `<story>#uat-<n>` or `events.attestation.test_id` orphans signed attestations (the data-loss path) — keep both verbatim; the rename is vocabulary + identifiers + labels only.
**Pinned-test churn.** Id-list / fixture tests break when identifiers move — update them in the same commit (real-build-typecheck-wall lesson).
**Cosmetic residue.** A missed 'UAT test' string in prose is harmless, not load-bearing.
**Fallback if blocked:** if the heading question can't be settled, ship identifier + prose + label renames only and leave the heading as `## Story UAT` — a clean partial with no parser risk.

title: One element per signal
description: The forest world is the observability layer rendered: each observed signal gets its own distinct art element, and an island's complexity emerges from the stack — never from a single "complexity" score.
body:
**The principle.** In the `#/tree` forest world, every observed signal renders as its OWN distinct art element; an island's complexity EMERGES from the stack of those elements, never from a single "complexity" score.

## Why

This is the rendering-side counterpart to `observability-first` (the data side: every state change is a typed event). Prettiness is not meaning — a substrate made to *look* intricate carries no fact, and a single complexity number is the wrong shape because there is no one value an island's form should map to. An island is intricate because the story it represents genuinely has more going on, and each thing it has is drawn by its own element. Without this rule, every new element re-litigates "what does the look mean?", elements get overloaded with two facts at once, and the world drifts from a faithful read of observed signals into decorative noise the studio can no longer be trusted to render.

## How to apply

Keep the encoding channels **orthogonal**: LOCATION is the DAG's job (rank, dependencies, load-bearing centring — ADR-0036); FORM is the stack of signal→element mappings; the SUBSTRATE (the tile ground) is the deliberately signal-less canvas whose only job is to host the meaningful elements gracefully at any size. Current bindings: **capabilities → island size**, **tests → plants/flora**, **state → the central tree**, **proof verdict → hue** (ADR-0040), **presence/builds → wisps**. Run the test on every new observable fact: a new fact ⇒ add a new distinct element — do NOT overload an existing one or invent a composite "complexity" reading. Keep each signal→element a cleanly-factored renderable, but do NOT build a pluggable "world-models" abstraction until 2–3 real models actually exist (factor the seam out when it is earned, not in anticipation).

title: Retire the generated apps/studio/data/assets.json
description: Delete the committed, generated `apps/studio/data/assets.json` and re-home its consumers, so no static generated file stands in for DB-backed artifact state — anything that needs the corpus wakes the live DB or derives from the structured `knowledge.json` seed instead. Parked as a proposal (owner steer 2026-06-16: deferred behind other in-flight work); the offline-backend fork (A/B/C below) is chosen at kickoff.
body:
**The proposal.** Remove the committed, generated `apps/studio/data/assets.json` — a drift-prone derivative of DB-backed artifact state — and re-home its four consumers (templates → `packages/core`; the migration; the offline studio backend; a WARN-only CLI count), honouring the principle that DB-backed data is woken from the live store, not read from a static file.

## Motivation

`assets.json` is a GENERATED derivative of `knowledge.json` (rendered by `apps/studio/data/build-corpus.mjs`) that duplicates artifact state the live Cloud SQL store now owns canonically (ADR-0023). Nothing gates its freshness, so it has already drifted: as of 2026-06-16 it holds 119 non-template units vs `knowledge.json`'s 124 (pattern 12 vs 15, principle 34 vs 36) — a parallel guidance-curators session edited the seed without regenerating, and nothing caught it. It is also the sole reason a latent inconsistency exists: `build-corpus.mjs` emits a `provenance` field into each asset, the live schema REJECTS `provenance`, and `load-corpus.ts` silently strips it before upsert — a round-trip that only exists because of the file. The hosted/default studio already does NOT read it (`selectedStore()` defaults to `pg` → `PgBackend`); `assets.json` only re-enters runtime through the offline JSON backend, the migration's template seed, the studio Playwright UAT, and a WARN. The cost of NOT acting is a permanently-stale committed file that three different docs already cite at three different stale counts (88 / 81 / 129), eroding trust in the seed.

## The change

Verified consumer map (each confirmed against code, not the brief) and its before→after:

1. TEMPLATES (the one hard dependency). `load-corpus.ts` reads ONLY the 10 `template`-category artifacts from `assets.json`; the structured kinds already come from `knowledge.json`. 9 of the 10 (`template-definition/principle/pattern/guardrail/techstack/process/open-question/agent/proposal`) are fully regenerated from `generateTemplate(kind)` + `KIND_SPECS`, both exported from `@storytree/core`; only `template-adr` is bespoke ~1 KB content. → Re-home all 10 into a new `packages/core` module (e.g. `templates.ts`) that synthesises the 9 generated ones and holds `template-adr` as a literal. `load-corpus.ts` imports it directly instead of reading `assets.json`. This also deletes the `provenance` round-trip bug.

2. THE MIGRATION (`packages/store/src/load-corpus.ts:63`). → Drop the `assets.json` read; pull templates from the new core module; keep reading `knowledge.json` for the structured units (unchanged).

3. THE OFFLINE JSON STUDIO BACKEND (`apps/studio/server/libraryBackend.ts` `JsonBackend`, active only under `STORYTREE_STUDIO_STORE=json`). This is the linchpin and the A/B/C fork — see Readiness. It is NOT merely a dev fallback: `apps/studio/uat/story-uat.spec.ts` pins the server to it (`STORYTREE_STUDIO_STORE=json`) and uses `assets.json` as the live read/write store (asserting byte-equality), so it is the studio organism's deliberate DB-free UAT seam (ADR-0010 §5's mock-UAT allowance).

4. THE CLI WARN (`packages/cli/src/commands.ts:183` `generatedAssetCount`, surfaced by `storytree library --check`). → Delete it: it exists ONLY to detect `assets.json` staleness, so it is pointless once the file is gone. Drop the matching `health.ts` count-reconciliation check + its tests.

5. GLOSSARY GENERATION STAYS. `build-corpus.mjs` generates BOTH `assets.json` AND `docs/glossary.md`; the glossary path reads `knowledge.json` only, so it survives independently. → Slim `build-corpus.mjs` to glossary-only (delete the `buildAssets` half), or split it into `build-glossary.mjs`.

UNCHANGED: `knowledge.json` (the structured seed, ADR-0018), the hosted/default `PgBackend` studio, `pnpm gate`/CI (never read or hard-checked it), and `build:claude` / `storytree agents` / the generated CLAUDE.md region (read `knowledge.json` via `loadCorpus`).

## Scope

Files touched: `packages/core/` (new `templates.ts` + export); `packages/store/src/load-corpus.ts` (drop the assets read); `packages/cli/src/commands.ts` + `health.ts` + their tests (drop the WARN); `apps/studio/data/build-corpus.mjs` (slim to glossary-only); `apps/studio/server/libraryBackend.ts` + `serveApi.integration.test.ts` + `apps/studio/uat/story-uat.spec.ts` (per the chosen A/B/C option); the deleted `apps/studio/data/assets.json` itself; and prose sweep (`CLAUDE.md`, `docs/glossary.md` line 266, `apps/studio/README.md`, `README.md`, and the ~6 `stories/studio` + `stories/library` specs that bake `assets.json` in). Stored data: none in the live DB changes (templates are already migrated; this only changes where a FUTURE re-seed sources them). Explicit NON-goals: do NOT touch `knowledge.json` as the structured source, the live Postgres store as canonical state, the hosted studio, or the glossary's status as a generated view. Manifest-safe: `assets.json` is not listed in `repo-manifest.json` and is nested (not a root surface file), so deleting it needs no manifest edit.

## Migration plan

1. Reserve an ADR at kickoff (`pnpm db:up` then `pnpm storytree adr new --title "Retire the generated assets.json" --pg`, ADR-0050 allocator — never hand-pick; latest on disk is 0064) recording the decision and the chosen A/B/C option. 2. Add `packages/core/templates.ts` (9 generated via `generateTemplate` + a `template-adr` literal); `pnpm --filter @storytree/core test` green. 3. Point `load-corpus.ts` at the core templates, drop the `assets.json` read; prove the migration still loads all 10 templates (offline `InMemoryStore` parity test). 4. Apply the chosen offline-backend option (A/B/C). 5. Delete the `generatedAssetCount` WARN + its tests. 6. Slim `build-corpus.mjs` to glossary-only; run it and confirm `docs/glossary.md` is byte-identical. 7. Delete `apps/studio/data/assets.json`. 8. Prose sweep. 9. `pnpm gate` green (per-package locally if the box OOMs; CI is authoritative) + a studio smoke. Route the provable pieces (templates-in-core, load-corpus parity, the backend change) through the inner loop where each has an isolatable red→green test.

## Readiness

Run in a quiet window (deferred 2026-06-16 behind other in-flight work). The decision still open at kickoff is the offline JSON backend's fate — pick ONE: (A) FULL RETIRE — delete `JsonBackend` + `STORYTREE_STUDIO_STORE=json`; the studio always uses the live DB (db:up / ADR-0049 hosted self-wake / the Start-DB banner); re-author the studio UAT against the DB (loses the offline-runnable acceptance proof, now needs credentials) and drop the JSON cases from `serveApi.integration.test.ts`. Purest reading of the principle, highest churn. (B) RECOMMENDED — DERIVE the offline view from `knowledge.json`: delete the committed `assets.json` but keep the offline backend, deriving its corpus from `knowledge.json` (rendered via core) on read, with create/edit/delete writing to a gitignored runtime file (seeded on first run; satisfies the UAT's cold-restart durability step). Honours the principle (no committed generated derivative stands in for DB data) while preserving the offline studio + its DB-free UAT seam. (C) KEEP IT, STOP IT ROTTING — do not remove it; add a gate check that regenerates and diffs (fail on stale) and fix the `provenance` bug. Cheapest, rejects the removal ask — only if the offline UAT seam is judged worth more than the cleanup. Steps 1–2, 5–8 (templates-to-core, drop the WARN, slim build-corpus, delete the file) apply to A and B regardless; only step 4 differs by option.

## Risks

The studio UAT seam is the main hazard: a naive `rm assets.json` leaves the offline studio rendering an empty Library and breaks `story-uat.spec.ts` (and the `stories/studio/*` UATs that shadow it) — which is exactly why the offline-backend fork (A/B/C) must be decided, not assumed. `serveApi.integration.test.ts` exercises `JsonBackend` and IS in the offline gate (`pnpm -r test`), so option A must update it. Secondary: a half-done re-home (templates moved to core but `load-corpus` still reading the file) silently drops templates on the next re-seed — gate each step green and add an `InMemoryStore` parity test asserting all 10 templates load. Tertiary: dangling `asset:`/`doc:` references after the prose sweep — run the referential-integrity check (ADR-0026). Low-risk on the live DB: templates are already migrated, so deleting the file changes only a future re-seed's source, not current state.

title: Solar-system world: central wiring hubs (cli/store) with every connection visible
description: Reframe the #/tree observability world so the wiring layer (cli, store) sits at the centre as hub nodes and EVERY cross-package connection — including to the hubs — is visible, de-noised visually rather than hidden.
body:
**The proposal.** Show the FULL architecture in the world: cli + store as central hub nodes that every organism connects to, with ALL edges rendered (the dense hub-spokes at low visual salience, e.g. very thin) — a radial 'solar system' layout rather than a pure tree — so badly-architected or agent-introduced coupling is visible at a glance instead of hidden.

## Motivation

The world's job is observability: to SEE the real architecture. The boundary-gate discussion (ADR-0074) surfaced that cli and store connect to nearly every organism. Hiding those connections (the rejected 'composition roots are exempt' idea) removes exactly the signal we most want — if an agent wires something wrongly, or a brownfield system is badly architected, that must be VISIBLE, not invisible. A tree/forest metaphor tangles when a few nodes connect to everything; a radial hub-and-spoke (solar-system) layout expresses 'these are central, everything orbits them' cleanly, and lets the dense hub-spokes be de-emphasised (thin, low-salience) instead of dropped.

## The change

Today: #/tree renders a forest of story-trees with dependency edges (roads, ADR-0073); the wiring layer (cli/store) is not represented as nodes at all. Proposed: cli and store become first-class central hub nodes; every organism declares its full connection surface (to other organisms AND to the hubs, owner call 2026-06-19 — one structured declaration per organism); every such edge is drawn; the layout shifts toward radial/solar-system with the hubs at the centre and organisms orbiting; the dense hub-spokes render at low visual salience so the graph stays readable while staying COMPLETE. Open fork: whether this replaces the forest metaphor wholesale or is a hub-centric LAYOUT realised within it (roads converging on a central clearing).

## Scope

Touches apps/studio's #/tree world (layout + edge rendering + the new hub nodes) and the data feeding it (/api/tree + the per-organism connection declarations it shares with ADR-0074's gate). Preserves the existing per-signal art mappings (ADR-0062 one-element-per-signal: plants=tests, etc.) — the LAYOUT and the hub nodes change, not the signal vocabulary. Does NOT decide the gate's enforcement model (ADR-0074) beyond rendering the same declarations. Non-goal: removing the forest's existing signals.

## Migration plan

1. Settle the data model with ADR-0074: each organism owns one structured declaration of its cross-package connections (the gate enforces it; the world renders it). 2. Add cli/store as hub nodes in the /api/tree payload. 3. Prototype the radial/hub layout behind a flag (default off, byte-identical when off — the house pattern for world changes) and de-noise the hub-spokes. 4. Operator-attested appearance review (frontend-builder two-stage proof: geometry red-green + owner screenshot-nod). 5. Flip the default on the owner's nod.

## Readiness

Depends on ADR-0074's ownership/declaration model landing first (the world renders what the gate declares). Best built by the frontend-builder inner-loop agent. Forest-world is a hot surface — coordinate via the notice board. Decision still open with the owner: solar-system reskin vs hub-layout-within-the-forest.

## Risks

A large departure from the current forest/roads aesthetic (ADR-0073) with real investment behind it — may land better as a hub-centric layout WITHIN the forest than a wholesale solar-system reskin; settle that fork before building. Hub-and-spoke still tangles if every edge is full-salience — the de-noising (thin/low-salience spokes) is load-bearing, not optional. Keep two concerns separable: the LAYOUT change (this proposal) and the gate's declaration model (ADR-0074).

title: consuming surface
description: A node that wires organisms together to deliver an experience — an apps/* app or the public-website subrepo — and is consumed by nothing: a SINK at the top of the dependency order, a first-class node in the observability/boundary graph rather than an exempt wiring layer.
body:
**In one line.** A consuming sink at the top of the dependency order — an app or the public subrepo — that wires organisms together and is itself depended on by nothing, yet is a first-class, enforced node in the boundary graph, not an exempt wiring layer.

## What it is

A **surface** is a CONSUMING node in the cross-story dependency graph: an `apps/*` application or the public-website subrepo that composes reusable organisms into an experience. It is a **sink** — it sits at the top of the dependency order and nothing depends on it (no inbound edge, never `foundational`). Its OUTBOUND code edges are covered, rendered, and enforced exactly like an organism's: every `@storytree/*` dependency must be a declared cross-story edge in the surface's own story `depends_on`, so the surface's true wiring is visible in the world and a miswiring is a red gate. The rule that puts a surface in the graph is **visibility-over-exemption generalised past packages**: the observability world's whole job is to show coupling, so the wiring/app layer is the signal you most want, not noise to hide (ADR-0074 §1/§2, extended to consuming surfaces by ADR-0100). A second-order property follows by construction: **bringing a surface into the scan forces its latent cross-story tangles to the surface** — the studio's hidden `studio-cloud → studio → studio-members → studio-cloud` cycle only became visible once the studio app was scanned, and was then resolved by the dependency-direction test, not tolerated (asset:cross-story-dependency). The evidence mechanism differs by surface kind: an `apps/*` app's edge is a package import the boundary scan covers; the public subrepo's edge is a synced build artifact whose liveness is proven by a drift gate (it consumes the built output, never the source), so it is a declared story node, not a scanned package.

## What it is not

Not an `organism` (asset:boundary): an organism is a REUSABLE bounded context other stories depend on (it draws inbound edges and may be `foundational`); a surface is consumed by no one. Not an exempt 'composition root' or 'just glue': the temptation is to hide the most-connected wiring nodes to reduce noise — rejected, because hiding them hides the most architecturally important relationships (the same call ADR-0074 §2 made for the cli/store hubs). Not a new kind of dependency EDGE: a surface's outbound edges obey the unchanged cross-story dependency-direction and no-cycle rules (asset:cross-story-dependency) — `surface` names the kind of NODE, not a different edge semantics.
provenance: Graduated (ADR-0095) from ADR-0100, which settled with the owner on 2026-06-24 that the boundary/observability graph must include consuming surfaces (apps + the public subrepo), not just organism packages. Distils the durable essence ADR-0074 §1/§2 left implicit (visibility-over-exemption stated only for packages) and ADR-0100 made explicit for surfaces: the sink-at-the-top node kind, the same-rule-as-an-organism coverage of its outbound edges, and the surfacing-a-surface-surfaces-its-latent-cycles property (the studio/studio-cloud/studio-members cycle). Consumers: story-author (classifies a surface and wires its depends_on), session-orchestrator / librarian-curator (reason about the observability world). The behavioural rule it rides on for cycle resolution is asset:cross-story-dependency.
